novelty_rationale: Short-term rental research focuses on contemporaneous price effects; this paper uses a supply-side instrument and spatial equilibrium framework to trace the long-run demographic and public goods consequences of platform-driven neighborhood transformation.
nn_title: Do short-term rental platforms affect housing markets? Evidence from Airbnb in B
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: How does the introduction of algorithmic dynamic pricing by large multifamily landlords affect rent levels, rent volatility, and tenant welfare, and does coordinated pricing constitute anticompetitive behavior?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the adoption timing of revenue management software (YieldStar, LRO) by multifamily operators across U.S. markets, with a structural model of landlord pricing to test whether observed price correlations exceed competitive benchmarks.
data: CoStar multifamily asking rents and vacancy data matched to software vendor customer lists (from litigation discovery), ACS rent-to-income ratios, and lease-level transaction data from participating property managers, covering 2010–2022.
key_finding: Adoption of algorithmic pricing likely increases effective rents by 3–7% in concentrated submarkets, reduces rent volatility for landlords by 20–30%, and generates consumer welfare losses of $800–$1,500 per unit annually, with coordination effects most pronounced when multiple competing landlords use the same software.
novelty_rationale: This is the first academic paper to empirically identify the causal rent impact of multifamily revenue management software adoption using quasi-experimental timing variation, directly informing ongoing antitrust litigation and regulatory debate about algorithmic pricing coordination.
nn_title: Structural change in the US office market after 2019: Evidence from lease‐level
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: What are the wealth effects of historical redlining on current homeowner equity accumulation, and how much of the current racial wealth gap can be attributed to compounded underinvestment in formerly redlined neighborhoods?
method: Counterfactual simulation using boundary discontinuity estimates of the redlining price discount linked to a dynamic wealth accumulation model, calibrated with demographic homeownership rates and price appreciation data from 1940–2020.
data: HOLC neighborhood security maps linked to census tract-level property value histories from historical assessor records, ACS contemporary housing equity estimates, and HMDA loan performance data for 200 U.S. cities.
key_finding: Compounded underinvestment from redlining likely accounts for 15–30% of the current racial housing wealth gap, with the largest effects in cities where redlined boundaries remain spatially persistent due to infrastructure investment patterns and school district boundaries that reinforced initial devaluation.
novelty_rationale: Existing redlining papers estimate contemporaneous price discontinuities but do not trace the compounded wealth channel over 80 years; this paper provides the first dynamic wealth accounting of how initial redlining-induced undervaluation propagated into the current racial wealth gap.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: How do local land use regulations interact with housing market cycles to amplify boom-bust dynamics, and can quantifying the 'regulatory zoning multiplier' explain cross-city differences in price volatility?
method: Panel instrumental variables using staggered state preemption of local zoning authority as instruments for zoning stringency, with a New Keynesian housing model incorporating regulatory frictions to quantify the multiplier effect on price amplitude.
data: Wharton Residential Land Use Regulation Index panel, CoreLogic metro-level house price indices, building permit data, and state legislative records of zoning preemption bills, covering 300 U.S. metros from 1990–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in regulatory stringency likely amplifies house price boom amplitude by 25–40% and bust depth by 20–35% relative to elastic-supply cities, with the zoning multiplier being substantially larger than estimated in static models that ignore the endogenous interaction between regulation and market expectations.
novelty_rationale: While supply elasticity and housing cycles have been studied, no paper uses state preemption laws as instruments for local regulation stringency to identify the dynamic amplification mechanism, nor has the concept of a 'regulatory zoning multiplier' been formalized and quantified.
nn_title: Residential land use regulation and the US housing price cycle between 2000 and
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does the presence of community land trusts permanently remove land from speculative markets in ways that improve long-run housing affordability and neighborhood stability without inducing gentrification displacement?
method: Matched difference-in-differences comparing CLT-designated census tracts to propensity-matched control tracts over 20-year horizons, with synthetic control methods for cities with large-scale CLT programs, examining prices, demographics, and displacement.
data: National CLT Network property records linked to county assessor transaction data, ACS longitudinal demographic microdata, and IRS migration data for all U.S. cities with established CLT programs, 1990–2022.
key_finding: CLT designation likely moderates long-run house price growth by 15–25% relative to comparable market-rate neighborhoods while simultaneously reducing displacement of low-income residents by 20–35%, suggesting CLTs represent a scalable land policy tool with measurable affordability and stability benefits.
novelty_rationale: Existing CLT research examines short-run price spillovers in single cities; this is the first multi-city, long-horizon study to identify whether CLTs permanently restructure land market dynamics or merely delay speculative pricing, using synthetic control methods for causal inference.
nn_title: The impact of local residential land use restrictions on land values across and
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: How does the revelation of neighborhood-level environmental hazard information through systematic government disclosure programs affect housing prices, demographic sorting, and environmental justice outcomes?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting the rollout of EPA's EJSCREEN environmental justice mapping tool and state-level toxic site disclosure laws, with heterogeneous effects by neighborhood income and racial composition.
data: EPA EJSCREEN cumulative exposure scores linked to CoreLogic transaction records, HMDA origination data, ACS demographic microdata, and state environmental disclosure law adoption dates for all U.S. census tracts, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Systematic environmental hazard disclosure likely reduces prices by 3–8% in high-exposure minority neighborhoods and triggers 5–12% income sorting as informed middle-income households exit, worsening environmental justice outcomes by concentrating environmental burdens on lower-income households who lack mobility.
novelty_rationale: While individual Superfund site studies exist, no paper has studied the systematic price and sorting effects of government-mandated aggregate environmental burden disclosure using quasi-experimental rollout variation, with explicit attention to whether disclosure helps or harms minority communities.
nn_title: Evidence of the Dual Nature of Property Value Recovery Following Environmental R
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: What is the causal effect of large-scale housing voucher portability reforms on neighborhood quality for voucher holders, surrounding property values, and local public service capacity?
method: Difference-in-differences using the staggered implementation of HUD's Moving to Work waiver program and Small Area Fair Market Rent reforms as instruments for voucher portability, tracking residential outcomes and neighborhood-level property markets.
data: HUD administrative voucher program records linked to IRS long-form migration and earnings data, HMDA property price data, school quality test score databases, and local government fiscal data for participating Moving to Work agencies, 2000–2022.
key_finding: SAFMR implementation likely increases voucher holder access to high-opportunity neighborhoods by 15–25% and improves children's long-run earnings by 3–7%, while generating modest positive property value spillovers of 1–3% through maintenance improvements, without measurable negative fiscal effects on destination communities.
novelty_rationale: Voucher portability reforms are among the most actively debated housing policies, yet no study has used the staggered SAFMR rollout as a quasi-experiment to trace both residential sorting outcomes and property market externalities simultaneously across receiving neighborhoods.
nn_title: Housing demand and neighborhood choice with housing vouchers
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: How do demographic aging and the intergenerational transfer of housing wealth affect local housing market turnover, price dynamics, and the concentration of housing wealth among younger cohorts?
method: Instrumental variable approach using cohort-level mortality shocks driven by the COVID-19 pandemic as exogenous variation in housing wealth transfer timing, linked to local housing market turnover and price outcomes via spatial panel models.
data: CDC excess mortality data by age and county linked to county assessor transaction volumes and prices, SCF wealth data, CoreLogic deed transfer records identifying estate sales, and ACS housing equity microdata, covering 2015–2023.
key_finding: A 10% increase in elder mortality-driven housing releases likely increases local turnover by 4–8% and moderates price growth by 2–4% in supply-constrained markets, while the concentration of transferred housing wealth among high-income heirs widens wealth inequality rather than broadly improving affordability.
novelty_rationale: The massive intergenerational wealth transfer through housing has been discussed but never causally analyzed; using pandemic mortality shocks as instruments for the timing of estate-driven housing releases provides the first clean identification of how demographic aging shapes housing market supply dynamics.
nn_title: Old homes, externalities, and poor neighborhoods. A model of urban decline and r
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does the geographic proximity of manufacturing reshoring and semiconductor plant investments affect local housing markets through labor demand shocks, and how quickly do housing supply responses moderate price appreciation?
method: Event study and difference-in-differences exploiting the announcement and construction phase of CHIPS Act-subsidized semiconductor fabrication plants and IRA-driven EV battery factory investments, comparing housing outcomes in treatment versus matched control counties.
data: Site Selection Group facility announcement database linked to CoreLogic county-level housing prices, building permit records, BLS local area unemployment statistics, and ACS commuting zone demographic data, covering 2020–2025.
key_finding: Major manufacturing plant announcements likely increase housing prices by 5–12% within 30 miles within 12 months of announcement, with supply response moderating long-run price effects by 30–50% in counties with elastic zoning, and persistent price premiums of 3–7% in supply-constrained Sunbelt counties.
novelty_rationale: Industrial policy-driven employment shocks are transforming specific regional housing markets in real time, yet no academic paper has studied the housing market dynamics of reshoring investment using the current wave of CHIPS and IRA facility announcements as clean identification events.
nn_title: Amazon is coming to town: Sequential information revelation in the housing marke
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Does the legalization of accessory dwelling units (ADUs) causally reduce housing costs in surrounding neighborhoods, or do the productivity and amenity spillovers from densification offset affordability gains?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting the sequential passage of ADU-permissive zoning ordinances across California cities between 2017 and 2023, with a heterogeneous treatment effects estimator (Callaway-Sant'Anna) and spatial lag controls for spillovers.
data: California Department of Housing and Community Development ADU permit records, CoreLogic deed and assessor data for all California residential parcels 2010–2023, and Zillow ZORI rent indices at the ZIP code level.
key_finding: ADU legalization is likely to reduce rents by 2–6% within a half-mile radius in supply-constrained cities, but the effect may be attenuated or reversed in high-amenity neighborhoods where densification attracts higher-income residents.
novelty_rationale: Prior ADU studies are descriptive or rely on single cities; a statewide causal design with spillover modeling and welfare decomposition between renters and owners is new.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: How do flood-zone reclassifications under FEMA's Risk Rating 2.0 reform capitalize into residential property prices, and do effects differ by owner race and income in ways consistent with differential information access?
method: A regression discontinuity design exploiting the sharp boundary between properties newly assigned higher flood-risk ratings and those just below the threshold, combined with heterogeneity analysis by census-tract demographics.
data: FEMA National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP) policy and claims data, FEMA Risk Rating 2.0 premium schedules released in 2021, CoreLogic deed transaction data 2019–2023, and HMDA loan application records for buyer demographics.
key_finding: Properties crossing into higher-risk tiers likely experience 5–12% price discounts, but minority and low-income owners may show smaller immediate price adjustments, consistent with lower awareness of new risk ratings and fewer refinancing options.
novelty_rationale: Risk Rating 2.0 is the first comprehensive overhaul of NFIP pricing in decades and creates a clean natural experiment that no published study has yet exploited for equity analysis.
nn_title: Who Bears Flood Risk? Evidence from Mortgage Markets in Florida
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does remote work adoption permanently shift the relative price gradient between urban cores and suburban rings, or does the gradient partially revert as firms enforce return-to-office mandates?
method: Dynamic event-study regressions using firm-level return-to-office announcements as shocks, linked to residential price gradients estimated from repeat-sales indices at the ZIP-code-to-CBD-distance level within each metro area.
data: Kastle Systems office occupancy data, Revelio Labs job-posting data capturing remote-work flexibility by employer, Zillow and CoreLogic repeat-sales indices at ZIP code level 2018–2024, and LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics for commute flows.
key_finding: Each 10-percentage-point increase in mandated return-to-office compliance likely steepens the price-distance gradient by 1–3%, partially but not fully reversing the pandemic-era suburbanization premium.
novelty_rationale: Most remote-work studies examine the initial shock; linking firm-level RTO mandates to dynamic price gradient recovery with employer-worker matching data is methodologically novel.
nn_title: The remote work revolution: Impact on real estate values and the urban environme
assigned_label: Pandemics, Disease, and Urban Property Markets

research_question: Do algorithmic rent-setting platforms (e.g., RealPage) cause supracompetitive rent coordination among multifamily landlords in concentrated markets, and what is the consumer welfare cost?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing rent growth in markets where RealPage adoption among large landlords crosses a critical mass threshold against comparable markets below the threshold, instrumented by the staggered rollout of RealPage's YieldStar product.
data: CoStar multifamily rent and vacancy data 2010–2023, RealPage client adoption records obtained via litigation discovery documents (publicly filed), Census ACS rental cost data, and NMHC apartment survey data.
key_finding: Markets where RealPage adoption exceeds 30% of large-unit inventory likely experience rent premiums of 3–8% above fundamentals, implying annual consumer welfare losses of hundreds of millions of dollars in affected metros.
novelty_rationale: Despite active antitrust litigation, no peer-reviewed causal study quantifies the rent effect of algorithmic coordination using market-penetration thresholds as the identifying variation.
nn_title: Rent Control, Rental Housing Supply, and the Distribution of Tenant Benefits
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: How does municipal fiscal stress — measured by pension funding gaps — affect local land-use liberalization decisions, and does debt-driven densification produce different housing supply outcomes than ideology-driven reform?
method: Instrumental variables using the interaction of pension plan vintage (defined-benefit vs. defined-contribution adoption timing) and actuarial return-rate assumptions as instruments for fiscal stress, with upzoning decisions and permit issuance as outcomes.
data: Public Plans Database (Boston College Center for Retirement Research) for pension funding ratios 2001–2022, Lincoln Institute of Land Policy zoning atlas, Census Building Permits Survey, and ICMA municipal fiscal survey data.
key_finding: Fiscally stressed municipalities are likely 15–25% more likely to approve upzoning within five years, but fiscal-stress-driven rezonings generate fewer actual housing completions due to simultaneous cuts in permitting capacity.
novelty_rationale: The link between municipal pension liabilities and land-use liberalization as a revenue strategy is theoretically plausible but empirically unexplored.
nn_title: Property values and pension underfunding in the local public sector
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Do mortgage lenders price climate transition risk — the risk that fossil-fuel-dependent local economies will decline under decarbonization — into interest rates and loan approval decisions?
method: Cross-sectional and panel regression of HMDA mortgage terms on county-level fossil fuel employment shares and projected job-loss estimates under IEA net-zero scenarios, controlling for current credit risk, collateral, and lender fixed effects.
data: HMDA loan-level records 2010–2023, Bureau of Labor Statistics Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for fossil fuel sector employment, IEA and NREL county-level energy transition exposure indices, and CoreLogic LTV and appraisal data.
key_finding: Lenders likely apply a modest but statistically significant 10–25 basis point spread on mortgages in high fossil-fuel-exposure counties, but the effect is concentrated among portfolio lenders rather than GSE-eligible loans, suggesting regulatory arbitrage.
novelty_rationale: Climate-risk pricing in mortgages has focused on physical risk (floods, fires); transition risk exposure through local labor markets is novel and policy-relevant given the Inflation Reduction Act's industrial transformation.
nn_title: When climate meets real estate: A survey of the literature
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does proximity to federally qualified community health centers (FQHCs) generate measurable house price premiums, and does this health-amenity capitalization vary by neighborhood income and insurance coverage rates?
method: Hedonic price regression with FQHC fixed effects and an event study around new FQHC openings, using a matched control group of census tracts that applied for but did not receive FQHC designation grants.
data: HRSA FQHC location and grant award data 1990–2022, CoreLogic property transaction records, Census ACS neighborhood income and insurance coverage, and CMS Medicare cost reports for FQHC service utilization.
key_finding: FQHC openings likely increase nearby home values by 2–5% in low-income neighborhoods but may have null or negative effects in higher-income areas where stigma or traffic concerns dominate.
novelty_rationale: Health-care access as a housing amenity is understudied relative to schools and transit, and the FQHC grant competition provides a credible quasi-experimental design not previously exploited.
nn_title: Spillover effects of human service facilities in a racially segmented housing ma
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: How do short-term rental (STR) platform bans and regulations affect long-term rental supply and rents, distinguishing between units converted back to long-term rental and units exiting the rental market entirely via owner-occupancy or demolition?
method: Synthetic control and difference-in-differences exploiting city-level Airbnb and VRBO bans or strict licensing regimes enacted between 2015 and 2022, with the decomposition of rental supply responses by unit type using ACS and administrative permit data.
data: AirDNA STR listing panel data 2014–2023, Zillow rental listings and ZORI indices, Census ACS 1-year estimates for rental vacancy rates, city administrative STR licensing databases (publicly posted), and CoStar apartment data.
key_finding: STR bans return approximately 30–60% of previously listed units to long-term rental use but reduce total housing supply by 5–15% through accelerated condo conversion and owner move-in evictions, yielding ambiguous net rent effects.
novelty_rationale: Existing STR regulation studies focus on aggregate rent effects; decomposing supply-side margins (STR-to-rental vs. STR-to-owner-occupied vs. demolition) is a key policy question that has not been answered empirically.
nn_title: Airbnb or not Airbnb? That is the question: How Airbnb bans disrupt rental marke
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the racial composition of a REIT's property portfolio affect its cost of capital and institutional investor ownership, reflecting either ESG preferences or statistical discrimination in capital markets?
method: Panel regression of REIT yield spreads, institutional ownership share, and analyst coverage on portfolio-level racial composition indices constructed from property locations crossed with neighborhood demographics, with REIT and time fixed effects.
data: SNL Real Estate / CoStar REIT property holdings data, CRSP/Ziman REIT financial data 1993–2023, Thomson Reuters 13F institutional holdings, and Census tract racial composition from decennial censuses and ACS.
key_finding: REITs with higher portfolio exposure to majority-minority neighborhoods likely face 20–40 basis point higher yield spreads and lower institutional ownership, a gap that narrowed but did not close after the 2020 ESG-driven investor focus.
novelty_rationale: REIT capital market discrimination has been theorized but not empirically quantified; linking property-level neighborhood demographics to cost-of-capital metrics via portfolio construction is methodologically novel.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How does the transition from LIBOR to SOFR affect the refinancing behavior of commercial real estate borrowers with floating-rate debt, and did the benchmark switch systematically redistribute wealth between lenders and borrowers?
method: Difference-in-differences comparing prepayment, default, and refinancing rates for CMBS loans indexed to LIBOR versus those indexed to prime or fixed rates around the 2021–2023 LIBOR transition, exploiting the exogenous regulatory deadline.
data: Bloomberg CMBS loan-level data, Trepp CMBS loan performance database 2018–2024, Federal Reserve H.15 benchmark rate series, and Mortgage Bankers Association commercial mortgage origination surveys.
key_finding: Borrowers with LIBOR-linked floating-rate CMBS loans likely experienced differential cash flow shocks during the transition period, increasing refinancing risk by 8–15% relative to fixed-rate counterparts, with losses concentrated in office and retail sectors.
novelty_rationale: LIBOR transition effects on CRE borrowers have been discussed in practitioner literature but not rigorously estimated in peer-reviewed research despite the availability of complete loan-level CMBS data.
nn_title: Changing Character of the Real Estate Mortgage Markets: Discussion
assigned_label: Securitization, Risk Retention, and Intermediation

research_question: Do inclusionary zoning (IZ) mandates reduce overall housing production sufficiently to offset their affordable unit production, and does this trade-off depend on local market tightness?
method: Synthetic difference-in-differences using staggered IZ adoptions across jurisdictions in the NCDB and HUD IZ inventory, with market tightness (vacancy rate, price-to-rent ratio) as a continuous moderator.
data: HUD Inclusionary Zoning National Database, Census Building Permits Survey 1990–2022, Furman Center / Lincoln Institute IZ policy tracking, ACS housing vacancy and rent data, and CoreLogic home price indices.
key_finding: IZ mandates likely reduce permitted market-rate units by 10–20% in tight markets (vacancy < 3%) while producing negligible market distortions in slack markets, implying a net affordability trade-off that is highly heterogeneous across cities.
novelty_rationale: While IZ effects on production are debated, no study uses the full national IZ database with a credible synthetic control design and explicitly models market tightness as a moderator.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Can machine-learning-based automated valuation models be shown to produce systematically lower appraisals for homes in Black and Hispanic neighborhoods even when controlling for all physical and locational characteristics, and what features drive the disparity?
method: Train gradient boosting and neural network AVMs on non-race-aware features using Fannie Mae's national appraisal dataset, then decompose residual racial valuation gaps using Shapley value attribution to identify which features act as proxies for race.
data: Fannie Mae Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) with over 50 million appraisals 2011–2022 (accessible via research partnership), Census tract demographics, and Freddie Mac automated valuation model outputs disclosed in SEC filings.
key_finding: AVM residuals are likely to show a 3–7% undervaluation in majority-minority tracts even after physical controls, driven primarily by neighborhood composition features (school ratings, crime indices) that serve as racial proxies.
novelty_rationale: Prior appraisal bias studies use hedonic OLS; a Shapley-decomposition approach applied to the UAD to identify which AVM features transmit racial bias is methodologically novel and directly actionable for regulators.
nn_title: Racial and ethnic price differentials in the housing market
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does wildfire smoke exposure — distinct from direct fire destruction — reduce housing demand and prices in affected communities, and how do these effects persist across multiple fire seasons?
method: Panel fixed-effects and distributed lag models regressing ZIP-code-level housing transactions and median prices on satellite-derived PM2.5 smoke exposure days, exploiting wind-driven variation in smoke plume paths as an instrument for local smoke exposure.
data: EPA's Hazard Mapping System smoke plume data and AirNow PM2.5 monitor readings 2006–2023, CoreLogic deed transaction and price data, Zillow listing data, NOAA wind direction and speed records as instruments, and Redfin days-on-market data.
key_finding: Each additional 10 smoke-affected days per year likely reduces transaction volumes by 5–8% and prices by 1–3% within the year, with partial recovery after clean-air seasons, suggesting smoke constitutes a distinct and persistent housing market risk.
novelty_rationale: Climate-risk housing research overwhelmingly focuses on floods and direct fire damage; wildfire smoke as an independent chronic amenity disamenity with quasi-random wind-path variation is a novel and underdeveloped identification strategy.
nn_title: Risk externalities and the problem of wildfire risk
assigned_label: Environmental hazards and property valuation

research_question: How do high-frequency changes in mortgage interest rates affect the housing market 'lock-in effect' — where existing homeowners are deterred from selling by their below-market rate mortgages — and what is the implied deadweight loss from reduced mobility?
method: Construct a homeowner-level panel of mortgage rate lock-in gaps (current rate minus prevailing rate) using HMDA origination data matched to Freddie Mac mortgage performance data, and estimate hazard models of listing and sale on the lock-in gap.
data: HMDA loan-level origination records 2000–2023, Freddie Mac Single Family Loan-Level Dataset, Zillow listing panel data, CoreLogic property transaction records, and Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey for weekly rate benchmarks.
key_finding: A 1 percentage point increase in the lock-in gap likely reduces annual listing probability by 10–18% per homeowner, implying a national inventory suppression of 800,000–1.5 million homes during the 2022–2024 rate spike.
novelty_rationale: While lock-in has been described theoretically, linking individual origination-rate histories to subsequent listing hazards with a nationwide matched dataset provides the first individual-level causal estimate of mobility deadweight loss.
nn_title: Mortgage Lock‐In, Mobility, and Labor Reallocation
assigned_label: Mortgage markets, policy, and household behavior

research_question: Does the construction of new market-rate housing in low-income neighborhoods reduce rents for nearby low-income renters, or do filtering and gentrification pressures dominate at different time horizons?
method: A dynamic difference-in-differences with stacked cohorts using the opening dates of new market-rate apartment buildings as treatment events, estimating rent effects at 6-month intervals from −24 to +60 months at the block level.
data: CoStar construction and lease-up data for new multifamily buildings 2010–2020, Zillow Observed Rent Index at ZIP and tract levels, Census ACS median gross rent, Opportunity Insights neighborhood income data, and HUD Fair Market Rents.
key_finding: New market-rate construction likely reduces nearby rents by 3–6% within two years as supply effects dominate, but neighborhoods with high initial low-income shares may experience rent increases after five years as amenity and gentrification spillovers accumulate.
novelty_rationale: The filtering vs. gentrification debate has been addressed in a few cities; a national stacked cohort design with dynamic horizons long enough to capture both mechanisms simultaneously resolves the key empirical ambiguity.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do commercial real estate cap rates incorporate sea-level rise risk priced at levels consistent with physical climate models, or is there systematic mispricing relative to projected inundation timelines?
method: Hedonic cap rate regression on NOAA sea-level rise inundation probability over 10, 30, and 50-year horizons for coastal commercial properties, comparing implied discount rates for climate risk against the rates embedded in FEMA flood insurance premiums.
data: CoStar transaction cap rates for coastal commercial properties 2000–2023, NOAA Digital Coast sea-level rise and inundation probability data, FEMA NFIP commercial policy premium data, First Street Foundation flood risk scores, and MSCI/IPD commercial property index data.
key_finding: CRE cap rates likely reflect only 20–40% of the climate-model-implied risk premium for 30-year inundation exposure, with the largest mispricings in markets with active redevelopment pipelines where short seller horizons suppress risk internalization.
novelty_rationale: CRE climate risk pricing studies are rare relative to residential; benchmarking cap rate risk premiums against physical model outputs and insurance pricing to quantify mispricing magnitude is novel.
nn_title: Is the Risk of Sea Level Rise Capitalized in Residential Real Estate?
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does public housing demolition under HOPE VI improve or worsen long-run economic outcomes for displaced residents, and are effects heterogeneous by the replacement housing type they receive (voucher, mixed-income, or nothing)?
method: Instrumental variables using HUD's competitive HOPE VI grant award decisions (exploiting near-miss applicants as controls) linked to individual longitudinal earnings, incarceration, and housing stability outcomes from administrative records.
data: HUD HOPE VI grant database 1992–2010, HUD administrative public housing tenant records linked via research data center to IRS earnings records (available via HUD-IRS data agreement), state incarceration records, and National Directory of New Hires wage records.
key_finding: Displaced residents receiving Housing Choice Vouchers likely show 8–15% higher 10-year earnings relative to those receiving no housing assistance, while residents relocated to mixed-income sites show intermediate effects, suggesting the housing safety net design matters more than demolition per se.
novelty_rationale: Prior HOPE VI studies lack the grant-competition instrument and the tripartite replacement-type comparison; linking HUD administrative data to IRS earnings via the existing HUD-IRS research agreement enables a complete welfare accounting.
nn_title: Long-term earnings and employment effects of housing voucher receipt
assigned_label: Housing policy effectiveness and targeting outcomes

research_question: How does the entry of institutional single-family rental (SFR) investors into a local market affect homeownership rates, house prices, and neighborhood racial composition over a 10-year horizon?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences using the geographic targeting algorithm of large SFR acquirers (Invitation Homes, American Homes 4 Rent) as revealed by their post-2012 zip-code-level acquisition intensity, with lagged institutional ownership share as the treatment variable.
data: CoreLogic deed transaction data identifying institutional buyers by entity name 2010–2023, Census ACS homeownership rates and racial composition, Zillow home price indices, HUD-USPS vacancy data, and HMDA mortgage origination records for first-time buyers.
key_finding: High institutional SFR penetration (top quartile) likely reduces local homeownership rates by 1–3 percentage points over five years, with disproportionate displacement of would-be Black and Hispanic first-time buyers in Sun Belt metros.
novelty_rationale: While SFR institutional investor effects have been studied in short windows, a 10-year panel exploiting zip-code targeting algorithms to construct an instrument for acquisition intensity with racial decomposition is substantially more rigorous.
nn_title: Do Wall Street Landlords Undermine Renters’ Welfare?
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Do Opportunity Zone designations increase property investment and reduce poverty in targeted census tracts, or do they primarily generate tax benefits for investors while inducing gentrification that displaces low-income residents?
method: Regression discontinuity exploiting the poverty-rate and median-income eligibility thresholds for Opportunity Zone designation, with separate analyses for commercial investment (CoStar), residential prices, and resident income mobility.
data: IRS Opportunity Zone eligible tract list, CoStar commercial construction and transaction data 2018–2023, CoreLogic residential price and transaction data, Census ACS tract-level poverty and income data, and Opportunity Insights intergenerational mobility estimates.
key_finding: Opportunity Zones likely increase commercial construction by 15–25% and raise residential prices by 4–8% near the eligibility cutoff, but poverty rates decline minimally as higher-income in-movers replace low-income residents rather than improving incumbent outcomes.
novelty_rationale: Though RD studies of OZ exist, none simultaneously models commercial investment, residential price effects, and incumbent resident poverty outcomes using the same design to decompose investment gains from displacement losses.
nn_title: Land Capitalization, Tiebout Mobility, and the Role of Zoning Regulations
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Does the information content of iBuyer algorithmic offers reveal systematic appraisal smoothing or price discovery failures in thin residential markets, and can iBuyer offer spreads serve as a real-time market liquidity index?
method: Compare iBuyer initial offer prices to subsequent market transaction prices and appraisal values using a matched sample, and construct a metro-level iBuyer spread index to test whether it leads traditional market price indices using Granger causality tests.
data: Zillow Offers and Opendoor transaction records reconstructed from CoreLogic deed data (iBuyer entity identification), Zillow and Case-Shiller price indices 2014–2022, FHFA appraisal-to-sale-price ratios from UAD data, and Redfin market heat index.
key_finding: iBuyer offer spreads likely lead traditional price indices by 4–8 weeks and widen sharply at market turning points, suggesting iBuyers internalize private information about liquidity risk that is not yet reflected in repeat-sales indices.
novelty_rationale: iBuyer pricing has been studied for bias but not as a price discovery mechanism or forward indicator; using entity-name matching in deed records to reconstruct iBuyer portfolios and compare to traditional indices is novel.
nn_title: Discount Brokerage in Residential Real Estate Markets
assigned_label: Real estate market intermediation and mechanisms

research_question: How do local eviction moratoriums during COVID-19 affect long-run landlord investment behavior, property maintenance, and subsequent rent levels, distinguishing between mom-and-pop and institutional landlords?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting variation in moratorium duration and strictness across cities and states, with landlord size as a moderator, using code violation filings and permit applications as proxies for maintenance investment.
data: Princeton Eviction Lab moratorium database, city-level building code violation administrative records (available from 20+ cities via open data portals), Census ACS rent data, CoStar rental market data, and HMDA small-balance multifamily loan origination data.
key_finding: Strict moratoriums likely reduce building permit applications by 15–25% and increase code violations by 10–20% among small landlords within two years, while institutional landlords show minimal maintenance response, suggesting differential capacity to absorb policy shocks.
novelty_rationale: Eviction moratorium research focuses on tenant outcomes; the landlord investment response — especially maintenance behavior using administrative code violation data and the institutional vs. small-landlord distinction — is empirically unexplored.
nn_title: Housing sales and construction responses to COVID‐19: Evidence from shelter‐in‐p
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the disclosure of broadband internet infrastructure quality systematically predict residential property price appreciation, and does the digital divide in broadband access contribute to diverging neighborhood trajectories?
method: Panel fixed-effects regression of ZIP-code-level house price growth on FCC Form 477 broadband availability and speed measures, instrumenting for broadband rollout using the location of pre-existing cable television franchise boundaries.
data: FCC Form 477 broadband availability data 2010–2022, Microsoft broadband usage speed data (released via NTIA partnership), CoreLogic home price indices at ZIP code level, Census ACS socioeconomic controls, and NTIA State Broadband Initiative coverage maps.
key_finding: A transition from no broadband to gigabit-capable infrastructure likely generates a 3–7% price premium over five years, with effects twice as large in rural areas, and broadband gaps explain 10–20% of the rural-urban divergence in housing appreciation since 2015.
novelty_rationale: Cable franchise boundaries as instruments for broadband rollout — borrowed from IO literature — have not been applied to housing markets, and the rural equity dimension of digital infrastructure capitalization is novel.
nn_title: Do House Price Levels Anticipate Subsequent Price Changes within Metropolitan Ar
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Are commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) special servicers systematically more likely to grant loan modifications to borrowers with stronger lender relationships, creating preferential treatment that distorts loss severity distributions?
method: Loan-level panel regression of modification probability and loss severity on relationship proxies (repeat borrower, same servicer as prior loan) conditional on borrower credit risk, property performance, and loan vintage, using special servicer fixed effects.
data: Trepp CMBS loan-level performance database 2000–2023 covering the full population of conduit CMBS loans, Bloomberg special servicer transfer records, CoStar property operating income data, and CMBS deal prospectuses for originator identity.
key_finding: Repeat borrowers with the same special servicer are likely 20–35% more likely to receive loan modifications and experience 15–25% lower realized losses, implying relationship rents that are borne by CMBS bond investors and raise systemic risk during downturns.
novelty_rationale: CMBS special servicer favoritism has been alleged by industry observers but never quantified with loan-level data; identifying relationship rents through repeat-servicer matches within the Trepp database is methodologically clean.
nn_title: Servicers and Mortgage‐Backed Securities Default: Theory and Evidence
assigned_label: Securitization, Risk Retention, and Intermediation

research_question: Does the spatial concentration of Section 8 Housing Choice Voucher holders induced by landlord participation patterns affect educational outcomes for voucher children compared to outcomes predicted by neighborhood quality alone?
method: Instrument for voucher concentration at the school-catchment level using variation in landlord participation driven by census-tract-level small-area fair market rent (SAFMR) thresholds, then compare peer-group effects on test scores to Moving to Opportunity estimates.
data: HUD administrative voucher holder records linked to school district boundaries via geocoding, Stanford Education Data Archive district-level test scores 1990–2019, HUD SAFMR schedules by zip code, National Student Clearinghouse college enrollment data, and NCES Common Core of Data.
key_finding: Voucher concentration above 15% of school enrollment likely reduces test score gains by 0.05–0.10 standard deviations relative to dispersed voucher placement, partially offsetting the neighborhood quality gains that motivate the voucher program.
novelty_rationale: SAFMR thresholds create a policy-induced instrument for within-district voucher concentration that isolates peer effects from selection, which prior Moving to Opportunity research cannot cleanly identify.
nn_title: Tiebout choice and universal school vouchers
assigned_label: School quality capitalization into housing prices

research_question: How do staggered increases in state and local transfer taxes affect housing market liquidity, turnover, and price levels, and do revenue gains offset deadweight loss from reduced mobility?
method: Regression kink design exploiting the non-linear rate schedules of real estate transfer taxes in multiple states, combined with difference-in-differences around rate increases, to jointly estimate price, volume, and tax revenue responses.
data: CoreLogic deed transaction data with sale prices 2000–2023, Lincoln Institute of Land Policy transfer tax rate database, state revenue department annual tax collection reports, Census ACS residential mobility rates, and HMDA mortgage origination volumes.
key_finding: A 1 percentage point increase in transfer tax rate likely reduces transaction volume by 8–14% and prices by 1–3%, with revenue elasticities below one, implying that the tax is largely self-defeating above modest rate levels and imposes mobility costs concentrated on moderate-income homeowners.
novelty_rationale: Transfer tax studies are rare in the U.S. context and typically examine single jurisdictions; a multi-state regression kink design that simultaneously estimates price, volume, and revenue responses to identify the full welfare accounting is novel.
nn_title: Revisiting the effects of housing transfer taxes
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Does the introduction of statewide rent control legislation cause commercial investors to reallocate capital from residential to commercial real estate within the same metropolitan area?
method: Difference-in-differences using staggered adoption of statewide rent control laws (Oregon 2019, California 2020) with synthetic control validation, measuring REIT portfolio reallocation and commercial transaction volumes.
data: CoStar transaction-level commercial data, REIT 10-K filings from SEC EDGAR, ZTRAX property transaction records, and HMDA mortgage origination data covering 2010–2023.
key_finding: Rent control adoption may cause 10–25% reallocation of institutional capital toward commercial assets in affected metros, with heterogeneous effects by property class and investor size.
novelty_rationale: Prior work focuses on rent control's effects on housing supply or rents in isolation; this paper identifies a novel cross-sector capital reallocation channel operating through investor portfolio decisions.
nn_title: Out of control: What can we learn from the end of Massachusetts rent control?
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Do property tax assessment lags create measurable wealth redistribution from renters to homeowners during periods of rapid house price appreciation?
method: Exploit variation in statutory assessment frequency and uniformity ratios across counties using a panel regression framework with county and year fixed effects, instrumenting for assessment timing with administrative reform dates.
data: Lincoln Institute of Land Policy property tax database, ATTOM Data Solutions assessment records, Census American Community Survey tenure and income data, CoreLogic house price indices, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Counties with longer reassessment cycles may show 5–15% lower effective tax rates for long-tenure homeowners relative to new buyers, systematically widening tenure-based wealth gaps during boom periods.
novelty_rationale: While assessment equity is studied descriptively, the causal link between assessment institutional design and wealth redistribution across tenure types during price booms has not been formally identified.
nn_title: Infrequent Assessments Distort Property Taxes: Theory and Evidence
assigned_label: Local tax policy and fiscal interdependence

research_question: Does the geographic clustering of environmental justice sites — such as Superfund locations, highways, and industrial facilities — interact nonlinearly to produce compounded housing price penalties beyond additive disamenity effects?
method: Spatial hedonic regression with interaction terms for co-location of multiple disamenity types, combined with machine learning (gradient boosted trees) to detect nonlinear price surface deformations near pollution clusters.
data: EPA Superfund and TRI facility location data, HPMS highway network data, CoreLogic deed-level transaction records for 40 major metros, American Housing Survey, 1990–2022.
key_finding: Neighborhoods bearing three or more environmental disamenities may face price discounts 30–50% larger than the sum of individual penalties, with disproportionate concentration in majority-minority census tracts.
novelty_rationale: Existing hedonic studies estimate disamenity effects individually; this paper tests for superadditive compounding effects and links them to environmental justice geography.
nn_title: Evidence of the Dual Nature of Property Value Recovery Following Environmental R
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: How does the entry of large technology firm campuses affect the within-city distribution of housing affordability, measured by housing cost burden, across different income quintiles?
method: Event study design using staggered announcements of major tech campus openings (Amazon HQ2, Apple Park, Google Sidewalk, etc.) as shocks, comparing price and rent trajectories within 5 km rings relative to synthetic control metros.
data: Zillow ZORI and ZHVI at ZIP code level, Census LODES commute data, BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages, CoStar office data, HUD CHAS affordability data, 2005–2023.
key_finding: Tech campus openings may increase housing cost burdens for bottom-quintile households by 8–18 percentage points within 5 km while generating moderate wealth gains for existing homeowners.
novelty_rationale: Existing work studies aggregate price effects of employment shocks; this paper examines distributional affordability consequences across the income distribution using a well-identified quasi-experiment.
nn_title: Amazon is coming to town: Sequential information revelation in the housing marke
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: Does lender exposure to the oil and gas sector predict tighter mortgage credit supply in energy-dependent housing markets during commodity price busts?
method: Instrument lender-level fossil fuel exposure with historical bank branch footprints interacted with energy employment share, then trace through to individual mortgage approval rates using HMDA, exploiting the 2014–2016 oil price collapse.
data: HMDA loan-level data, FDIC Call Reports for lender balance sheet composition, BLS energy employment by county, FRED oil price series, CoreLogic deed records, 2010–2020.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in lender fossil fuel exposure may reduce mortgage approval rates in energy counties by 4–9% following price busts, amplifying housing market downturns.
novelty_rationale: This paper connects bank-level fossil fuel exposure to mortgage credit supply contraction, revealing a financial amplification mechanism in energy-dependent housing markets beyond direct income effects.
nn_title: The Housing Market Impacts of Shale Gas Development: Corrigendum
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics and price determinants

research_question: Do urban tree canopy investments generate heterogeneous house price capitalization effects that systematically favor higher-income neighborhoods, worsening green gentrification?
method: Difference-in-differences using staggered municipal street tree planting programs in several U.S. cities, with parcel-level canopy data from USDA Forest Service urban tree inventories matched to deed-level sales.
data: USDA Forest Service Urban Tree Canopy assessments, city street tree inventories (NYC, Philadelphia, Los Angeles, Chicago), ZTRAX parcel transaction data, Census demographic data, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Tree canopy additions in low-income majority-minority neighborhoods may produce smaller or no price capitalization relative to high-income areas, while accelerating displacement through subsequent gentrification dynamics.
novelty_rationale: Prior hedonic studies estimate average canopy values; this paper identifies the distributional incidence of green infrastructure investment and its role in environmental gentrification.
nn_title: What Is a Tree Worth? Green‐City Strategies, Signaling and Housing Prices
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: How do changes in municipal zoning code complexity — measured by word count, restriction counts, and conditional use requirements — affect housing supply elasticity and long-run price levels?
method: Construct a novel panel index of zoning code complexity using natural language processing on zoning ordinances, then estimate its effect on housing permits and prices using two-way fixed effects with IV based on reform wave spillovers.
data: National Zoning Atlas and individual municipal zoning codes (text), Census Building Permits Survey, Zillow ZHVI, Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index, American Community Survey, 1990–2020.
key_finding: A 10% increase in zoning code complexity may reduce housing permit issuance by 3–7% and raise long-run price levels by 2–5%, with larger effects in jurisdictions lacking administrative capacity.
novelty_rationale: Existing land use regulation indices rely on surveys; this paper builds an objective text-based measure of regulatory complexity and identifies its causal effect on housing market outcomes.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do racial steering practices by real estate agents persist in the digital era, measurable through differential broker response rates and neighborhood referral patterns by buyer race?
method: Large-scale matched-pair audit experiment sending paired email inquiries from names with distinctly Black- and White-associated identities to randomly selected listing agents, measuring response rates, response content, and geographic steering.
data: Original audit experiment data collected via Zillow and Realtor.com public listings across 20 metros stratified by fair housing enforcement intensity, matched to neighborhood racial composition from ACS, 2023–2024.
key_finding: Black-named inquirers may receive 15–25% fewer responses and be steered toward neighborhoods with 20–30% higher minority shares, with smaller differentials in metros with active fair housing testing programs.
novelty_rationale: Prior audit studies use phone or in-person methods; this paper exploits large-scale digital audit methodology to identify steering at scale and test whether fair housing enforcement reduces digital-era discrimination.
nn_title: Restricted access: Real estate agent response to client race, ethnicity, gender,
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does REIT specialization versus diversification across property types affect return performance and crash risk, especially during sector-specific demand shocks like the COVID-19 pandemic?
method: Construct specialization indices for publicly traded U.S. REITs using property-type revenue shares, then estimate panel regressions of returns, volatility, and drawdowns on specialization, controlling for size, leverage, and market conditions.
data: SNL Real Estate / CoStar REIT financial database, NAREIT monthly return data, SEC 10-K property-type revenue disclosures, CRSP daily stock returns, 2000–2023.
key_finding: Highly specialized REITs may exhibit 20–35% larger drawdowns during sector-specific shocks but generate alpha during expansions, suggesting specialization creates an option-like return profile.
novelty_rationale: REIT diversification versus specialization has been studied in small samples; this paper uses the COVID shock as a clean identification event and links specialization explicitly to crash risk dynamics.
nn_title: Granular risks and stock returns: Evidence from commercial real estate
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: How does the spatial mismatch between affordable housing locations and job accessibility for low-wage workers affect long-run employment and earnings outcomes for voucher holders?
method: Exploit random assignment variation in housing voucher location from the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment and subsequent voucher lottery programs, combined with LEHD longitudinal earnings data to trace labor market outcomes by commute shed access.
data: HUD Moving to Opportunity public use files, HUD MTCS voucher administrative data, Census LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics, EPA Smart Location Database job accessibility measures, 2000–2020.
key_finding: Voucher placements in neighborhoods with high low-wage job accessibility within 30-minute transit commutes may raise annual earnings by $1,500–$4,000 relative to lower-accessibility placements.
novelty_rationale: MTO research has focused on neighborhood quality broadly; this paper isolates the job accessibility channel and quantifies its earnings returns, informing optimal voucher mobility counseling.
nn_title: The Effects of Housing Assistance on Labor Supply: Evidence from a Voucher Lotte
assigned_label: Spatial mismatch and racial employment inequality

research_question: Does the construction of luxury condominiums in transit-accessible urban cores reduce transit ridership by altering the neighborhood income composition and mode preferences of new residents?
method: Difference-in-differences using building permit issuance for luxury condos near rail stations as a treatment, comparing station-level boardings before and after project completion with station-year fixed effects.
data: APTA/NTD station-level ridership data, CoStar and Zillow building permit and sales data, Census ACS commute mode choice data, OpenStreetMap transit network, 2005–2022.
key_finding: Luxury condo construction near stations may reduce transit ridership by 3–8% as high-income residents drive rather than use transit, partially offsetting the density benefits of transit-oriented development.
novelty_rationale: Transit ridership and housing development are typically studied separately; this paper identifies a novel behavioral mechanism linking housing luxury market development to transportation demand at the station level.
nn_title: The capitalization of metro rail access in urban housing markets
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: Do natural disaster declarations systematically trigger CMBS loan modifications and deferrals, and does this forbearance behavior differ between bank-held and securitized commercial mortgages?
method: Match FEMA disaster declaration data to property-level CMBS loan performance data using address geocoding, then estimate hazard models of default and modification rates comparing CMBS vs. bank-held loans using difference-in-differences.
data: Trepp CMBS loan-level performance database, FFIEC bank examination data on CRE loan modifications, FEMA disaster declarations geocoded to county level, CoStar property data, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Securitized loans in disaster-affected counties may be 2–4 times more likely to enter special servicing relative to bank-held loans of similar characteristics, reflecting servicer incentive misalignment in CMBS structures.
novelty_rationale: CMBS servicer behavior during disasters has received limited academic attention; this paper identifies an agency conflict between special servicers and bondholders that amplifies commercial real estate distress after natural disasters.
nn_title: Retraction of: Mortgage Finance and Climate Change: Securitization Dynamics in t
assigned_label: Natural disasters and real estate market dynamics

research_question: Does the presence of active neighborhood association governance — HOAs and community benefit districts — affect within-neighborhood socioeconomic sorting and reduce racial integration over time?
method: Exploit the staggered formation of HOAs and Business Improvement Districts using administrative HOA registration data, tracking decadal Census demographic and income outcomes within governed versus non-governed neighborhoods.
data: State HOA registration records (Florida, Texas, California, Nevada), IRS Form 990 filings for nonprofit BIDs, Census decennial demographic data and ACS, CoreLogic property ownership records, 1990–2020.
key_finding: HOA establishment may reduce neighborhood racial entropy by 5–10 percentage points over 10 years and increase income sorting, suggesting private governance reinforces residential stratification.
novelty_rationale: While HOA price premia are documented, the causal effects of HOA governance formation on long-run demographic sorting and racial integration have not been rigorously identified.
nn_title: Segregation and Tiebout sorting: The link between place-based investments and ne
assigned_label: Racial residential segregation patterns and causes

research_question: How does the increasing prevalence of build-to-rent (BTR) single-family communities affect adjacent existing home values and neighborhood demographic composition?
method: Spatial difference-in-differences matching BTR community openings to comparable neighborhoods without BTR development, using parcel-level transaction data with a one-mile ring design and propensity score matching on pre-treatment characteristics.
data: CoStar and Yardi Matrix BTR property universe, ZTRAX deed-level transaction records, Census ACS block group demographics, Zillow ZHVI, 2015–2023.
key_finding: BTR community openings may depress adjacent single-family sale prices by 2–6% while generating modest rental rate increases, with heterogeneous effects by local vacancy rate and income level.
novelty_rationale: BTR is a rapidly growing and understudied asset class; this paper provides the first quasi-experimental evidence on the neighborhood externalities of purpose-built single-family rental communities.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does access to residential Property Assessed Clean Energy (PACE) financing increase energy efficiency retrofits, and do the resulting improvements capitalize into home values?
method: Exploit county-level rollout of PACE programs using a staggered difference-in-differences design, measuring retrofit rates and subsequent sale price effects using hedonic models that control for energy performance disclosure requirements.
data: PACENation loan origination data, California PACE public disclosure records, DOE Home Energy Score database, CoreLogic sale transaction data, Zillow property characteristics, 2008–2022.
key_finding: PACE availability may increase retrofit rates by 15–30% and generate a 2–5% sale price premium, with capitalization concentrated in energy-cost-sensitive markets with high utility prices.
novelty_rationale: PACE effects on retrofit rates and price capitalization are unclear due to selection concerns; this paper uses program rollout timing as an instrument to jointly identify take-up and hedonic value effects.
nn_title: The capitalization of energy efficiency: Evidence from the housing market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do foreclosure auctions systematically underprice properties relative to subsequent arms-length market transactions, and does underpricing vary by neighborhood racial composition in ways consistent with discriminatory investor targeting?
method: Compare foreclosure auction sale prices to subsequent resale prices within 24 months for the same property, regressing the discount on neighborhood racial composition with controls for property condition, holding period, and investor type.
data: ATTOM foreclosure auction records, CoreLogic deed-level resale transactions, Home Mortgage Disclosure Act data, Census demographic data, county assessor records for property characteristics, 2005–2020.
key_finding: Foreclosure auction discounts may be 8–15 percentage points larger in predominantly Black and Hispanic neighborhoods, suggesting systematic underpricing that facilitates wealth extraction from minority communities.
novelty_rationale: While foreclosure concentrations in minority neighborhoods are well documented, the mechanism of systematic auction underpricing as a racial wealth extraction channel has not been formally tested.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does the geographic concentration of student loan debt affect local housing demand, homeownership rates, and price levels in college towns and high-education metros?
method: Use the Federal Reserve Bank of New York Consumer Credit Panel data aggregated to the ZIP code level to construct local student debt exposure measures, then estimate effects on homeownership transitions using a panel IV strategy based on pre-existing college proximity.
data: FRBNY Consumer Credit Panel (ZIP-level aggregates), Census ACS homeownership rates, HMDA mortgage originations, Zillow ZHVI, IPEDS college enrollment data, 2005–2022.
key_finding: A 10% increase in local average student debt burden may reduce homeownership rates among 25–34 year-olds by 1.5–3 percentage points and suppress demand-side price growth in starter home segments.
novelty_rationale: Individual-level studies of student debt and homeownership exist, but the geographic spillover effects of concentrated student debt on local housing market dynamics have not been examined.
nn_title: A Simple Model of Subprime Borrowers and Credit Growth
assigned_label: Regional variation in mortgage market structure

research_question: How does mandatory energy performance disclosure at point of sale affect the price capitalization of energy efficiency and the rate of post-sale retrofitting across different regulatory designs?
method: Exploit staggered adoption of state and municipal energy disclosure mandates using a staggered difference-in-differences framework, measuring hedonic price effects of EPC ratings and subsequent building permit activity for energy improvements.
data: DOE Building Performance Database, state energy disclosure records (Austin, New York City, Washington state), CoreLogic transaction data, Census residential building permits, utility billing data from state programs, 2010–2023.
key_finding: Mandatory disclosure may increase the price premium for high-efficiency properties by 2–8% and raise post-sale retrofit permits by 10–20%, with larger effects in high-energy-cost regions.
novelty_rationale: Most energy disclosure studies use voluntary or single-jurisdiction data; this paper exploits cross-jurisdiction variation in regulatory design to identify disclosure effects on both prices and retrofit investment.
nn_title: The capitalization of energy efficiency: Evidence from the housing market
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Does the announcement and construction of urban gondola or aerial tramway transit systems generate differential property value capitalization compared to conventional rail transit investments of equivalent connectivity?
method: Hedonic difference-in-differences comparing property value trajectories near gondola versus light rail station announcements in comparable settings (Medellín, New York, Bolivia), exploiting announcement timing as the treatment event.
data: Global property transaction databases (DataQuick for U.S., government registro for Latin America), OpenStreetMap transit network data, World Bank urban data, satellite land use imagery, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Urban gondola systems may generate 50–70% lower property value capitalization than rail investments of equivalent travel time savings, consistent with stigma or land use integration differences.
novelty_rationale: Transit capitalization literature focuses exclusively on rail and bus; gondola systems offer a rare natural experiment comparing transit mode type holding connectivity roughly constant.
nn_title: The capitalization of metro rail access in urban housing markets
assigned_label: Transit infrastructure capitalization in urban housing

research_question: Do office-to-residential conversion subsidies and zoning variances actually increase net housing supply or primarily displace new construction by crowding out greenfield and infill development?
method: Exploit city-level variation in the timing and generosity of office conversion incentive programs using a synthetic control approach, measuring net housing permits by type (conversion, new construction) and price effects in the conversion building's submarket.
data: CoStar office and residential transaction data, Census Building Permits Survey, city planning department conversion permit records (New York, Chicago, San Francisco, Washington DC), Zillow price indices, 2015–2023.
key_finding: Office conversion subsidies may add 0.4–0.8 net housing units per converted unit after accounting for displacement of new construction, suggesting partial but incomplete supply additionality.
novelty_rationale: Policy debate on office conversion assumes full additionality; this paper tests the displacement hypothesis by examining whether conversion activity crowds out conventional residential development.
nn_title: Office Rent Processes: The Case of U.S. Metropolitan Markets
assigned_label: Commercial Real Estate Rent Dynamics

research_question: Does the spatial clustering of Airbnb listings in historic preservation districts reduce the residential population and alter the built environment's social fabric in ways measurable through nighttime light intensity and foot traffic?
method: Combine Airbnb listing density data with satellite-derived nighttime light intensity and SafeGraph foot traffic data at the census block level, using historic district boundaries as a regression discontinuity design.
data: AirDNA/Inside Airbnb listing panel data, NASA VIIRS nighttime light imagery, SafeGraph neighborhood foot traffic data, National Register of Historic Places district boundaries, ACS residential population, 2015–2022.
key_finding: Historic district blocks with above-median Airbnb density may show 10–20% lower nighttime residential light intensity and altered foot traffic timing toward daytime tourism patterns, indicating residential hollowing.
novelty_rationale: Short-term rental effects on housing supply are well studied, but this paper uses novel remote sensing and mobility data to test whether tourist clustering reshapes the social ecology of historic urban neighborhoods.
nn_title: Preserving history or restricting development?  The heterogeneous effects of his
assigned_label: Spatial Economics and Urban Land Values

research_question: How do pension fund real estate allocation decisions respond to funding ratio changes, and does this procyclical behavior amplify commercial real estate price cycles?
method: Construct a panel of state and local pension fund real estate allocations from CAFR filings, regress allocation changes on lagged funding ratios and CRE returns, then simulate the aggregate price impact using a structural model of investor demand.
data: Public Plans Data pension fund database (Boston College CRR), state CAFR filings on alternative asset allocations, NCREIF Property Index returns by property type, CoStar transaction volumes, 1990–2022.
key_finding: A 10-percentage-point decline in pension funding ratios may cause a 0.5–1.5 percentage point reduction in real estate allocation, generating procyclical selling that amplifies CRE price declines by 5–12%.
novelty_rationale: Pension fund behavior as a driver of CRE price cycles is theoretically argued but empirically unidentified; this paper provides the first panel evidence linking pension funding stress to real estate allocation dynamics.
nn_title: Real Estate in an ALM Framework: The Case of Fair Value Accounting
assigned_label: Real Estate Asset Valuation and Returns

research_question: Does hospital closure in rural and suburban markets cause measurable declines in nearby residential property values through reduced amenity access and neighborhood economic contraction?
method: Event study using staggered hospital closures since 2005, comparing price trajectories for properties within 5 miles of closed versus operating hospitals using a stacked difference-in-differences design with nearest-neighbor matched controls.
data: American Hospital Association Annual Survey for closures, CoreLogic deed-level transaction data, Medicare Provider of Services files for hospital characteristics, BLS county employment data, ACS demographic controls, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Hospital closures may reduce residential property values within 3 miles by 3–9% over five years, with larger effects in rural counties where the hospital represented a dominant local employer.
novelty_rationale: While health access effects of hospital closure are studied, the property value externality — capturing both amenity loss and local economic contraction — has not been rigorously quantified.
nn_title: Searching for Goldilocks: The Distance‐Based Capitalization Effects of Local Pub
assigned_label: Hedonic pricing and residential location choice

research_question: Do zoning variance and special permit approvals exhibit racial or income-based disparities in approval rates and processing times that systematically disadvantage minority developers and communities?
method: Compile administrative records of variance applications from multiple city planning departments, regress approval probability and processing time on applicant and neighborhood racial/income characteristics with controls for project type and zoning district.
data: Municipal planning department administrative records (Chicago, Los Angeles, Philadelphia, Atlanta variance databases), Census demographic data, CoStar ownership data on applicant firms, 2010–2022.
key_finding: Applications from majority-minority census tracts or applicants with minority-associated names may face 10–20% lower approval rates and 30–60 day longer processing times after controlling for project characteristics.
novelty_rationale: Racial disparities in land use regulation administration are theorized but rarely tested with administrative microdata; this paper provides quasi-experimental evidence on bureaucratic discrimination in planning decisions.
nn_title: Racial Discrimination and Redlining in Cities
assigned_label: Racial discrimination in housing markets

research_question: Does private equity ownership of multifamily apartment buildings lead to greater rent extraction, deferred maintenance, and tenant displacement relative to individual landlord ownership, and through what mechanisms?
method: Match private equity acquisition events identified from HMDA, deed records, and SEC disclosures to building-level outcomes including code violations, eviction filings, rent changes, and 311 maintenance complaints using a difference-in-differences design.
data: ZTRAX ownership transaction data matched to SEC private equity fund disclosures, NYC/Chicago/LA housing court eviction records, city 311 maintenance complaint data, HUD HMDA multifamily originations, Zillow Observed Rent Index, 2010–2022.
key_finding: Private equity acquisition may increase eviction filing rates by 20–40%, raise rents by 5–12%, and increase code violation complaints by 15–25% relative to comparable buildings under individual landlord ownership.
novelty_rationale: Institutional investor SFR effects are widely studied, but private equity ownership of multifamily rentals — a larger segment — lacks rigorous causal identification of its effects on tenant welfare and building maintenance.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Rental Markets, Policy Effects, and Housing Supply

research_question: Does the legalization of accessory dwelling units (ADUs) through statewide preemption of local zoning restrictions measurably increase housing supply and reduce rent growth in California relative to comparable markets?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting California's 2017–2020 ADU reform package as a staggered natural experiment, with synthetic control validation using border census tracts in Nevada and Arizona as counterfactuals.
data: California Department of Housing and Community Development ADU permit records (2013–2023), Census Bureau Building Permits Survey, Zillow Research rent indices, and CoStar multifamily rent data at the ZIP-code level.
key_finding: Statewide ADU legalization likely increased permitted ADU completions by 200–400% in permissive jurisdictions and modestly dampened rent growth by 2–5 percentage points, with larger effects in high-cost coastal metros.
novelty_rationale: While ADU policy is widely discussed, no study has rigorously estimated rent-level effects of the full California reform sequence using high-frequency administrative permit data and a credible synthetic control design.
nn_title: Making housing affordable? The local effects of relaxing land-use regulation
assigned_label: Land use regulation and property values

research_question: Do mortgage lenders price flood zone designation differently for properties owned by racial minorities compared to identically situated white-owned properties after controlling for structural and locational risk factors?
method: Regression analysis with granular fixed effects (census tract × year × flood zone × loan-to-value bin), using matched samples of minority and white borrowers within the same FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area boundary and exploiting within-boundary variation in lender behavior.
data: HMDA loan-level data (2018–2023) merged with FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer, First Street Foundation flood risk scores, CoreLogic property characteristics, and FFIEC lender CRA examination data.
key_finding: Minority borrowers in high-risk flood zones are plausibly charged 15–40 basis points more in interest and denied at 10–20% higher rates than observationally equivalent white borrowers, suggesting compound exposure to both climate and credit market discrimination.
