key_finding: A one-standard-deviation improvement in tract-level social-media sentiment is expected to predict 2–5% higher house price appreciation over the following two years, with effects concentrated in transitional neighborhoods near gentrification frontiers.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus contains a news-based uncertainty index and a housing sentiment index using social media, none constructs a place-specific emotional valence measure from user-generated content and tests whether collective affective perception of a neighborhood constitutes a priced amenity.
nn_title: Quantifying Sentiment with News Media across Local Housing Markets
assigned_label: Real Estate Market Liquidity and Efficiency

research_question: Do residential mortgage lenders systematically misprice flood risk in recourse versus non-recourse states, and does legal recourse reduce the moral hazard that drives under-pricing of physical climate risk?
method: Regression discontinuity at state recourse-law borders combined with a triple-difference comparing pre- and post-2012-FEMA-map-revision loans in flood zones versus control zones across recourse and non-recourse states, using loan-level interest rates as the outcome.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data (2008–2022), FEMA National Flood Insurance Program flood-zone shapefiles and map-revision dates, state recourse-law coding from prior legal literature, and First Street Foundation flood-risk scores.
key_finding: Non-recourse states are expected to show 20–40 basis points less flood-risk pricing in mortgage rates than recourse states for equivalent flood exposure, consistent with lender moral hazard when default losses are bounded.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies recourse lending effects on prices and non-recourse mortgage valuation, and separately examines flood risk capitalization, but no paper links the legal recourse environment to climate-risk mispricing in mortgage interest rates—a frontier connecting real estate law, mortgage economics, and climate finance.
nn_title: Who Bears Flood Risk? Evidence from Mortgage Markets in Florida
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: How does the spatial diffusion of co-working space supply affect the rent gradient and office vacancy structure of surrounding conventional office submarkets?
method: A dynamic spatial panel model with instrumental variables, where entry of WeWork/IWG locations serves as the treatment, instrumented by their HQ-network rollout sequence; outcomes are CoStar submarket rents, vacancy, and lease duration for conventional office within 0.25–2 miles.
data: CoStar submarket-level lease and vacancy data for 50 US metros (2010–2023), proprietary co-working operator location and desk-count data, and INRIX commute-flow data to construct submarket accessibility controls.
key_finding: Co-working entry is expected to reduce conventional office rents within 0.5 miles by 3–7% and increase vacancy by 1–3 percentage points, but improve rent resilience in submarkets with high remote-work exposure by providing demand flexibility.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies office market dynamics and rent determination extensively but does not treat co-working as an endogenous competitor that reshapes conventional market equilibria; this idea imports spatial competitive-interaction models from retail economics.
nn_title: Explaining Location Patterns of Suburban Offices
assigned_label: Commercial Office Market Dynamics and Pricing

research_question: Can continuous-time survival analysis applied to the entire universe of US residential listing histories identify whether algorithmic automatic price-reduction triggers by listing platforms shorten time-on-market without sacrificing sale price?
method: Mixed-proportional hazard model of listing-to-sale spells estimated on Zillow/Redfin listing histories, exploiting platform-specific rollout of automated price-reduction recommendation tools as a quasi-experiment, with sale-price-to-list-price ratio as a secondary outcome.
data: Full MLS listing histories from 30 metros (2015–2023) including timestamps of every price reduction and source (agent-initiated vs platform-recommended), combined with final transaction prices from deed records.
key_finding: Platform-triggered price reductions are expected to shorten time-on-market by 8–15 days while having a statistically zero impact on final sale prices, suggesting algorithmic nudges correct seller anchoring without imposing pricing costs.
novelty_rationale: The corpus thoroughly studies list price strategy, time-on-market, and anchoring, but no paper examines whether platform-mediated algorithmic nudges override seller behavioral biases, nor uses complete listing-event histories to identify this effect.

assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does municipal-level adoption of form-based zoning codes—which regulate building form rather than use—increase housing supply density, reduce construction permitting times, and lower house prices relative to conventional use-based zoning controls?
method: Difference-in-differences with stacked cohort design using the staggered municipal adoption of form-based codes across US cities, matching adopting to non-adopting municipalities on pre-treatment density, housing cost trajectories, and political characteristics.
data: Form-Based Codes Institute database of adoptions (2000–2023), Census Bureau Building Permits Survey, HUD CHAS affordability data, Zillow metro-level house price indices, and permit processing time data from municipal open-data portals.
key_finding: Form-based code adoption is expected to increase permitted multifamily units by 15–30% and reduce median permitting times by 20–40 days, translating into 3–6% lower house price levels over a five-year horizon.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies zoning density controls, exclusionary zoning, and land-use regulation extensively, but no paper distinguishes the causal effect of form-based versus use-based zoning reform on supply, permitting efficiency, and prices—an increasingly policy-relevant distinction.
nn_title: The local residential land use regulatory environment across U.S. housing market
assigned_label: Land use regulation and urban economic effects

research_question: Do REIT boards' human capital networks—measured by directors' scientific and engineering credentials outside real estate—predict superior climate-risk adaptation capital expenditures and lower future physical-climate-risk discounts to NAV?
method: Text-mining of BoardEx director biographies to construct a STEM-credentials index at the board level, combined with panel regressions of capital expenditures on resilience categories (flood barriers, HVAC upgrades) and NAV discounts on the STEM index, instrumented by peer-board director departures.
data: BoardEx director biographies for all US equity REITs (2000–2023), REIT 10-K capital expenditure disclosures coded by NLP into resilience versus routine categories, First Street climate-risk scores for REIT property portfolios, and SNL Financial NAV estimates.
key_finding: Boards with higher STEM credentials are expected to allocate 20–35% more capital to climate-resilience upgrades and face a 5–10% smaller NAV discount per unit of physical climate risk exposure, suggesting that human capital composition shapes climate strategy quality.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies REIT board governance and CEO compensation extensively, and separately examines climate risk transmission to equity returns, but no paper links board human capital composition—particularly scientific expertise—to climate-adaptive investment behavior.

assigned_label: Corporate Governance and REIT Firm Value

research_question: Can neuroimaging-derived measures of place attachment—obtained from fMRI responses to neighborhood images—predict deviation of homeowners' self-reported valuations from hedonic model predictions, and do these deviations translate into systematic overpayment at purchase?
method: Controlled laboratory experiment presenting neighborhood photographs to a sample of recent homebuyers while measuring fMRI neural activation in reward and identity-processing regions; place-attachment scores are then linked via survey to self-reported valuations and matched to hedonic model residuals from actual purchase transactions.
data: fMRI scans of 300 recent homebuyers across two metros, post-scan survey of self-reported valuations and purchase motivations, transaction records from county deed databases matched to CoreLogic hedonic model estimates.
key_finding: Higher neural place-attachment activation is expected to predict 5–12% positive deviation of self-reported from hedonic model valuations and a 3–7% overpayment relative to comparable sales, with stronger effects for buyers in gentrifying neighborhoods.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains one neurological study of strategic mortgage default and one experimental study of REIT trading using fMRI, but no paper applies neuroimaging to the home-purchase decision itself, bridging neuroscience, behavioral economics, and hedonic valuation in an entirely new way.
nn_title: Lender Characteristics and the Neurological Reasons for Strategic Mortgage Defau
assigned_label: Hedonic Pricing Models and Housing Valuation

research_question: Does the staggered rollout of utility-scale battery storage facilities create measurable property value spillovers, and do these effects differ systematically from those of conventional power plants because of distinct noise, visual, and fire-risk externalities?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences with heteroskedasticity-robust stacked regression, exploiting county-level permitting dates as quasi-random variation in facility location, augmented by a hedonic spatial discontinuity at parcel boundaries adjacent to facility setback zones.
data: National Renewable Energy Laboratory battery storage siting records merged with CoreLogic deed-level transaction histories (2010–2024) covering approximately 8 million arms-length sales within 5 km of storage sites across 40 US states.
key_finding: Battery storage facilities likely impose a modest negative price gradient of 1–4% within 500 meters, declining sharply with distance, with fire-incident events amplifying discounts by an additional 3–8%, while colocation with solar generating sites partially offsets stigma through green-amenity capitalization.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive wind-turbine and power-plant hedonic studies but entirely omits battery storage, whose externality profile combines novel fire risk, visual disamenity, and green-energy signaling in proportions unexamined by any existing real estate paper.

assigned_label: Environmental Contamination and Property Values

research_question: Does the staggered deployment of 5G small-cell infrastructure alter commercial real estate rents and capitalization rates by changing the marginal productivity of tenant businesses that are data-intensive, and does this effect vary by property type and urban density?
method: Two-way fixed-effects panel regression with an instrumental variable strategy using the pre-5G location of legacy 4G tower density interacted with federal spectrum auction timing as instruments, separating demand-driven from supply-side rent responses.
data: FCC antenna structure registration and spectrum auction databases merged with CoStar lease-level rent and vacancy records for office, retail, and industrial properties (2016–2024) across 50 US metropolitan areas.
key_finding: 5G rollout likely raises office rents by 2–6% in properties where small-cell density exceeds a threshold, with larger effects for technology-intensive tenants and negligible or negative effects for retail properties where disamenity from antenna placement offsets productivity gains.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains no study linking telecommunications infrastructure deployment to commercial lease pricing; this idea imports the industrial-organization literature on network externalities and productivity spillovers into a real estate context that has never been examined.
nn_title: Wireless Towers and Home Values: An Alternative Valuation Approach Using a Spati
assigned_label: Commercial Office Market Dynamics and Pricing

research_question: Does genetically predicted risk aversion—estimated via polygenic scores from biobank data linked to administrative property records—explain the residual variation in homeownership rates, mortgage leverage, and housing portfolio concentration that standard economic models leave unexplained?
method: Two-sample Mendelian randomization using polygenic scores for financial risk tolerance as instruments for measured risk preferences, applied to linked UK Biobank genomic data and Land Registry transaction records, with Hausman tests to distinguish genetic from environmental channels.
data: UK Biobank release 3 (N ≈ 500,000 with genome-wide array data) linked probabilistically to HM Land Registry Price Paid Data and English Housing Survey wealth modules covering 1995–2022.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in genetically predicted risk tolerance is likely associated with 4–9 percentage points higher homeownership probability and 8–15% higher loan-to-value ratios, with heritability of housing tenure explaining roughly 15–25% of the gap unexplained by income and wealth.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus includes a twin study on homeownership genetics, no paper uses polygenic scores and Mendelian randomization to causally identify the biological dimension of housing finance decisions, representing a frontier import from genomic economics.
nn_title: A Search for the Genetic Contributors to Strategic Mortgage Default: The Catecho
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Do machine learning-detected visual signals of neighborhood social disorder—graffiti, broken windows, and abandoned vehicles extracted from street-level imagery—predict mortgage default at the loan level above and beyond standard underwriting variables, and do lenders price this risk?
method: Computer vision models (ResNet-50 fine-tuned on labeled street imagery) produce property-level disorder indices that are merged with loan-level HMDA and CoreLogic performance data in a gradient-boosted competing-risks survival model, with Shapley value decomposition to quantify incremental predictive contribution.
data: Google Street View time-stamped images for 2.5 million US residential properties (2007–2022) linked to Fannie Mae/Freddie Mac Single-Family Loan Performance datasets covering 30 million loan-month observations.
key_finding: Visual disorder indices likely predict 90-day delinquency with an AUC increment of 0.02–0.04 beyond FICO and LTV, with the strongest effects in low-income census tracts, while mortgage rates show no corresponding premium, implying lenders systematically underprice this dimension of collateral risk.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive hedonic and default modeling but no study systematically extracts causal neighborhood physical-disorder signals from street imagery to predict mortgage default, combining computer vision, survival analysis, and fair-lending literatures in a genuinely new configuration.
nn_title: Imputing Borrower Heterogeneity and Dynamics in Mortgage Default Models
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: How does the staggered legalization of accessory dwelling units (ADUs) through state preemption of local zoning—controlling for concurrent supply policies—affect the price-rent ratio, housing tenure composition, and filtering rates for low-cost housing units in treated metropolitan areas?
method: Synthetic control with stacked difference-in-differences exploiting the 2017–2022 wave of state-level ADU preemption laws in California, Oregon, Washington, and Maine, instrumenting for actual ADU construction with pre-existing lot-size distributions that predict ADU feasibility.
data: Census Bureau Building Permits Survey, American Community Survey 5-year microdata (2010–2023), and Zillow Research transaction and rental listing files covering all 50 states with lot-size records from county assessor databases.
key_finding: ADU legalization likely reduces the price-rent ratio by 3–7% in treated ZIP codes after five years, with filtering effects concentrated in census tracts where existing single-family lots exceed median size thresholds, and tenure composition shifting toward renting among young households.
novelty_rationale: While ADU studies exist in the corpus, none estimates the general-equilibrium filtering and price-rent ratio effects of state preemption using a rigorous synthetic control design that separates legalization from construction, linking land-use law to housing market dynamics literature.
nn_title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evi
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Does the revelation of subsurface PFAS (forever chemical) contamination in groundwater—identified through EPA enforcement actions—reduce residential property values and alter mortgage origination patterns differentially by whether the property relies on private wells versus municipal water supply?
method: Difference-in-differences with event-study extension exploiting the staggered timing of EPA Maximum Contaminant Level announcements and state enforcement actions as shocks to local PFAS salience, interacted with parcel-level water-source classifications from county assessor data.
data: EPA Safe Drinking Water Information System and state environmental agency PFAS monitoring records linked to CoreLogic deed and assessor files (2010–2023) and HMDA mortgage origination data for approximately 3 million parcels within 5 km of confirmed contamination sites.
key_finding: PFAS revelation likely causes a 5–12% price discount for private-well properties within 1 km of contamination, with no significant effect for municipal-water properties, and a 15–25% reduction in conforming mortgage originations near affected private-well parcels, suggesting lenders internalize collateral risk before appraisers.
novelty_rationale: The corpus extensively covers Superfund and flood externalities but has no study on PFAS contamination, and no existing paper uses water-source heterogeneity to identify differential contamination capitalization and its simultaneous effect on both property values and mortgage credit supply.

research_question: Does the mandatory disclosure of building-level embodied carbon in construction permits—introduced through staggered municipal ordinances—generate a price premium for low-embodied-carbon structures that is distinct from operational energy efficiency premiums, and does this premium vary with investor versus owner-occupant buyers?
method: Hedonic regression with buyer-type interaction terms estimated on repeat-sale pairs straddling ordinance adoption dates, using a regression discontinuity in building permit dates as an instrument for embodied-carbon certification, with placebo tests on non-adopting neighboring cities.
data: Municipal building permit databases with embodied-carbon disclosures from Seattle, Portland, Boston, and Denver (2018–2024) linked to county deed records distinguishing institutional, investor, and owner-occupant purchases through LLC registry matching.
key_finding: Low-embodied-carbon structures likely command a 2–5% price premium for institutional buyers but an indistinguishable premium for owner-occupants, suggesting that institutional investors price transition risk while households discount long-horizon liabilities, with premia rising after each IPCC report publication.
novelty_rationale: The corpus has an extensive green-certification literature focused on operational energy use, but no study examines embodied carbon—a fundamentally different and unexamined dimension of building sustainability—as a distinct source of market value, importing concepts from lifecycle assessment into real estate economics.

research_question: Does the staggered introduction of automated real-time property tax reassessment systems—replacing triennial manual cycles—reduce assessment regressivity and alter homeowner mobility, renovation investment, and the supply of starter homes in affected jurisdictions?
method: Stacked difference-in-differences exploiting the rollout of algorithmic reassessment platforms across US counties (2012–2023), with triple-difference specifications comparing owner-occupants versus investors and high-appreciation versus low-appreciation neighborhoods, instrumenting renovation investment with permit records.
data: Lincoln Institute of Land Policy property tax database merged with county assessor roll microdata, CoreLogic deed history, and Census Bureau Residential Finance Survey for approximately 50 US counties that adopted automated valuation-based reassessment during the study period.
key_finding: Automated reassessment likely reduces assessment regressivity by 20–35% as measured by the coefficient of dispersion across value quintiles, while simultaneously reducing renovation investment by 8–14% in high-appreciation neighborhoods where tax liability becomes more responsive to improvements, creating a policy tradeoff the literature has not identified.
novelty_rationale: The corpus covers assessment equity and renovation investment separately, but no paper connects the technology of reassessment to both dimensions simultaneously, and none examines how algorithmic valuation platforms alter the behavioral incentives that standard property tax theory predicts.

research_question: How does the mandatory public disclosure of algorithmic pricing tools used by institutional landlords—required by the European Union's AI Act transparency provisions beginning 2025—affect rental price levels, price dispersion, and vacancy rates compared to markets without such requirements?
method: Synthetic control method using EU member states with and without early national AI Act transposition as treatment and control groups, with a stacked event-study design around country-specific enforcement dates and cross-validation using a placebo test on commercial leases where AI pricing transparency was not mandated.
data: Eurostat experimental rental price statistics and Numbeo crowdsourced rental listings (2020–2027) covering 250 European cities, linked to national rental platform market share data and ESMA AI tool registration filings for property management firms.
key_finding: Mandatory disclosure of algorithmic pricing likely reduces median asking rents by 2–5% and interquartile rent dispersion by 10–18% within 18 months of enforcement, as coordinated pricing equilibria unravel when landlords cannot credibly signal algorithmic uniformity, with larger effects in markets where a single platform held over 30% share.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains no causal study of algorithmic rent-setting regulation; this idea imports industrial-organization insights on algorithmic collusion and market design into real estate, constituting a genuinely novel policy-evaluation research program with direct regulatory relevance.
nn_title: Information Asymmetry, Regulations and Equilibrium Outcomes: Theory and Evidence
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Does the accumulation of deferred maintenance obligations on aging infrastructure—estimated from civil engineering condition indices—predict commercial real estate capitalization rate increases and debt distress in metropolitan areas before standard macroeconomic indicators signal stress, and can infrastructure grades serve as leading indicators for CMBS loss severity?
method: Panel predictive regression using American Society of Civil Engineers infrastructure report card grades as time-varying predictors of forward-looking CMBS delinquency and loss-given-default rates, instrumented by the political composition of state infrastructure appropriations committees to address endogeneity, with Granger causality tests on a 20-year horizon.
data: ASCE state-level infrastructure report card data (2001–2023) merged with Trepp CMBS loan-level performance database (3.5 million loan-quarter observations), BLS metro-area employment data, and FHWA bridge condition inventory covering 50 US states and 100 metropolitan areas.
key_finding: A one-grade deterioration in bridge and water infrastructure indices likely predicts a 15–30 basis point increase in CMBS loss severity over a 3-year horizon and a 0.4–0.8 percentage point rise in industrial and retail capitalization rates, with predictive power concentrated in secondary markets and exceeding that of standard credit cycle variables.
novelty_rationale: The corpus has no paper linking civil infrastructure quality to commercial real estate debt performance or cap rates; this idea opens a research program at the intersection of infrastructure economics and real estate finance that has received no attention despite its direct relevance to long-horizon investment risk.

research_question: Do large-scale language model embeddings of property listing text capture latent dwelling quality dimensions that systematically predict appraisal gaps, mortgage default, and long-run price appreciation beyond hedonic attributes?
method: Fine-tune a sentence-transformer model on Zillow/Redfin public listing descriptions to produce property-level semantic embeddings, then use these as regressors in hedonic, repeat-sales, and competing-risks mortgage termination models, instrumenting for listing endogeneity with agent fixed effects.

research_question: Do city council election cycles generate predictable political budget cycles in local building permit approvals that create systematic seasonality in new housing supply and house price dynamics?

research_question: Do zoning variance approvals granted by local boards exhibit political business cycles, with approval rates rising in election years, and does this cyclicality differentially inflate land values in municipalities with appointed versus elected zoning boards?

research_question: Does judicial assignment randomness in foreclosure proceedings—exploiting quasi-random judge assignment in states with specialized foreclosure dockets—affect the long-run neighborhood recovery trajectory of foreclosed properties, and through what intermediary (property condition, investor type, or resale speed)?

data: Florida, New York, and New Jersey court docket records with judge identifiers linked to individual foreclosure cases (available from state judicial portals), merged with ATTOM property distress data, CoreLogic deed records, and Census tract vacancy rates for 2008–2022.

research_question: Can the cross-sectional co-movement of REIT equity returns be systematically explained by the spatial overlap of underlying property portfolios, and does this spatial-overlap factor price the cross-section of REIT returns beyond standard Fama-French and real-estate factors?

research_question: Does the staggered legalization of accessory dwelling units across California jurisdictions generate measurable spatial spillovers on single-family home prices in adjacent non-ADU-permitting jurisdictions through household sorting and supply-side displacement?
method: Border discontinuity difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered passage of ADU legalization ordinances across California municipalities between 2016 and 2022, comparing single-family transaction prices and rental rates on each side of municipal boundaries in a 0.5-mile band.
data: California Bureau of Real Estate and CoreLogic deed transaction records at the parcel level (2010–2023), merged with ADU permit issuance data from California Department of Housing and Community Development, municipal ordinance passage dates, and ACS tract rental vacancy data.
key_finding: Single-family prices in municipalities adjacent to ADU-permitting jurisdictions plausibly decline 2–4 percent as households sort toward the ADU-permitting side, while rents in non-permitting adjacent municipalities rise 3–6 percent due to displaced renters, revealing cross-boundary welfare transfers from supply-restrictive zoning spillovers.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies ADUs descriptively and examines land-use deregulation impacts within treated areas, but no paper uses a border discontinuity design to identify cross-jurisdictional spillover effects of ADU legalization on prices and rents in neighboring non-permitting municipalities.
nn_title: Short-term rentals and the housing market: Quasi-experimental evidence from Airb
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Do bankruptcy judges' leniency toward commercial mortgage borrowers, identified through quasi-random judge assignment in Chapter 11 proceedings, affect commercial real estate market recovery and CMBS pool performance through renegotiation and value-transfer mechanisms?
method: Two-stage least squares using judge leniency index (constructed from leave-one-out mean reorganization rates per judge) as an instrument for loan workout outcomes in Chapter 11 filings, with outcomes measured as CMBS pool cumulative loss rates, property resale prices, and time-to-resolution.
data: PACER federal bankruptcy court filings with judge identifiers linked to commercial real estate loan cases (2008–2020), merged with Trepp CMBS loan-level performance data, CoStar commercial property transaction records, and FDIC failed-bank commercial real estate exposure data.
key_finding: Assignment to a lenient bankruptcy judge plausibly reduces CMBS pool realized losses by 8–12 percent through higher reorganization rates but extends workout timelines by 18–24 months, revealing a fundamental tradeoff between loss mitigation and resolution speed with implications for CMBS pricing models.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus examines CMBS default, servicer behavior, and foreclosure laws, no paper uses quasi-random bankruptcy judge assignment as an instrument to identify causal effects of judicial leniency on commercial mortgage workout outcomes and CMBS pool performance.
nn_title: Theory and Evidence on the Resolution of Financial Distress
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Does the physical distance between a household's primary residence and their workplace—instrumented by mass layoff events—causally affect subsequent mortgage default and prepayment decisions through labor income risk and residential lock-in channels?
method: Instrumental variables design using mass layoff events from the Bureau of Labor Statistics Mass Layoffs Statistics (and WARN Act notices) to instrument for changes in commute distance, estimating effects on mortgage delinquency and prepayment using a competing risks hazard model with property fixed effects.
data: FHFA loan-level mortgage performance data merged with BLS Mass Layoffs Statistics (2001–2019) and WARN Act employer-level layoff notices, matched by ZIP code to Census LODES workplace-residence distance data and CoreLogic property transaction records.
key_finding: A mass-layoff-induced 20-minute increase in commute distance plausibly raises 12-month default hazard by 15–25 percent among underwater borrowers and reduces prepayment by 30–40 percent due to residential lock-in, suggesting commute disruption is a quantitatively important but unmeasured trigger in the double-trigger default model.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies unemployment as a mortgage default trigger and homeownership as a labor market friction separately, but no paper uses mass layoff-induced commute disruptions as an instrument to jointly identify labor income and residential lock-in channels in mortgage termination decisions.
nn_title: Spatial Heterogeneity in Mortgage Terminations by Refinance, Sale and Default
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Do hospital closures—which permanently reduce local amenity value—generate persistent capitalization losses in surrounding residential real estate and, through declining collateral values, increase community bank mortgage delinquency rates in affected counties?
method: Stacked difference-in-differences exploiting the timing of 350+ U.S. hospital closures between 2010 and 2022, estimating effects on nearby house prices using CoreLogic repeat-sales data, then tracing downstream effects on community bank mortgage delinquency rates using FDIC Call Report data.
data: American Hospital Association Annual Survey hospital closure events (2010–2022) geocoded to census tracts, merged with CoreLogic deed and repeat-sales transaction data, FDIC Call Report county-level mortgage delinquency and bank balance sheet data, and ACS tract socioeconomic controls.
key_finding: Hospital closure plausibly depresses house prices within 2 miles by 4–8 percent over 3 years, with the effect concentrated in rural and low-income communities, and this collateral erosion translates into a 15–25 basis point increase in community bank mortgage delinquency rates in the affected county.
novelty_rationale: The corpus examines school quality, police funding, and retail closures as neighborhood amenity shocks, but no paper connects hospital closures—a salient and accelerating trend—to the dual outcomes of residential collateral value and downstream community bank credit risk through a unified causal framework.
nn_title: Foreclosure Externalities: Have We Confused the Cure with the Disease?
assigned_label: Foreclosure discounts and distressed property pricing

research_question: Does participation in the Low-Income Housing Tax Credit program by for-profit versus nonprofit developers lead to systematically different long-run physical maintenance outcomes, tenant stability, and surrounding property value spillovers?
method: Matched difference-in-differences comparing LIHTC properties developed by for-profit and nonprofit sponsors using HUD inspection scores as a maintenance outcome, instrumenting developer type with state-level nonprofit capacity measures, and estimating surrounding property price spillovers using a hedonic ring design.
data: HUD LIHTC database with developer type classifications and National Housing Preservation Database property records (1987–2022), merged with HUD REAC physical inspection scores, CoreLogic parcel transaction data for surrounding properties, and NCSHA state LIHTC allocation records.
key_finding: Nonprofit-developed LIHTC properties plausibly score 8–12 points higher on HUD physical inspections after 10 years, exhibit 15–20 percent lower tenant turnover, and generate 2–4 percent positive spillovers on surrounding property values compared to for-profit-developed properties, with the gap widening in distressed neighborhood contexts.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies LIHTC tenant benefits and affordable housing spillovers, but no paper exploits the for-profit versus nonprofit developer distinction with causal identification to trace long-run maintenance quality, tenant stability, and neighborhood spillover differences within the same federal program.
nn_title: Do Tenants Capture the Benefits from the Low‐Income Housing Tax Credit Program?
assigned_label: Property taxation effects on housing markets

research_question: Does the rollout of electric vehicle charging infrastructure across U.S. counties causally affect residential and commercial property values, and does this effect differ by property type, income level, and local grid carbon intensity?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the county-level timing of federal and state EV charging installation grants under the IIJA, combined with hedonic pricing models and a border discontinuity approach at county lines to isolate capitalization effects from correlated amenity demand shocks.
data: U.S. DOE Alternative Fuels Station locator database (geocoded EV charger openings 2010–2024), CoreLogic transaction-level residential and commercial property data, EPA eGRID carbon intensity data, and ACS county-level income and commuting data.
key_finding: Properties within 0.25 miles of newly installed public EV chargers likely appreciate 1–4% for residential assets in high-carbon-grid counties where EV adoption signals green gentrification, while commercial retail properties near chargers may see larger premiums reflecting dwell-time complementarity; income-stratified neighborhoods may exhibit divergent patterns consistent with green amenity sorting.
novelty_rationale: The corpus addresses green building certification and energy efficiency labels but entirely omits EV infrastructure as a real estate amenity, and no existing paper exploits the staggered federal grant rollout as a quasi-experiment linking transportation electrification to property markets.
nn_title: The Evolution of Green Building Amenities: The Case of EV Charging Stations
assigned_label: Environmental Contamination and Property Values

research_question: Do large language model-generated property descriptions in MLS listings systematically differ in sentiment and information density across racial and income composition of neighborhoods, and do these textual differences independently predict sale prices and time on market beyond standard hedonic controls?
method: Automated text analysis using transformer-based sentiment and information-content scoring applied to the full universe of MLS listing descriptions, linked to transaction outcomes via hedonic regression with neighborhood fixed effects, and a decomposition of price gaps attributable to listing-language quality differences across demographic strata.
data: Zillow Research's public listing feed or CoreLogic MLS data covering listing descriptions for millions of U.S. transactions (2015–2023), Census tract demographic data from ACS, and FHFA house price indices for control.
key_finding: Listings in majority-minority or low-income census tracts likely contain systematically shorter, lower-sentiment, and less information-rich descriptions, and this language gap may account for 1–3% of the unexplained price differential documented in fair lending literature, suggesting an overlooked channel of digital discrimination in property markets.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus includes audit studies of agent behavior and appraisal discrimination, no paper examines how algorithmically detectable textual asymmetries in listing language constitute a structural channel for racial price gaps, connecting NLP methods not yet represented in the corpus to fair lending questions.
nn_title: Asymmetric or Incomplete Information about Asset Values?
assigned_label: Racial Discrimination and Housing Market Prices

research_question: How does the sudden salience of building-level embodied carbon, triggered by the staggered adoption of whole-life carbon reporting requirements in European building codes, affect commercial property transaction prices and cap rates?
method: Difference-in-differences estimation exploiting the staggered country- and canton-level adoption of embodied carbon disclosure mandates across EU member states, matched to transaction-level commercial real estate data, supplemented by a triple-difference specification comparing high versus low embodied-carbon building vintages within treated markets.
data: MSCI/IPD pan-European property transaction database, national building energy performance certificate registries that include lifecycle carbon estimates (UK, Netherlands, France, Sweden), Eurostat construction vintage data, and EU member state regulatory adoption dates compiled from official legislative journals.
key_finding: Buildings with high embodied carbon in jurisdictions newly subject to disclosure requirements likely experience 3–8% price discounts relative to low-embodied-carbon comparables, with cap rate spreads widening for older, concrete-heavy structures, while new timber-frame and low-carbon-concrete buildings command growing certification premia consistent with a nascent brown discount distinct from the operational energy premium already documented.
novelty_rationale: The corpus documents operational energy efficiency premia (LEED, Energy Star) and climate transition risk in REITs but has not examined embodied carbon—the carbon locked into construction materials—as a distinct pricing factor, a gap of increasing policy relevance as lifecycle carbon accounting enters building regulation.
nn_title: Building Sustainability, Certification, and Price Premiums: Evidence from Europe
assigned_label: Green Building Features and Property Valuation

research_question: Does short-term rental platform market share (Airbnb listing density) in a neighborhood causally affect the volatility—not just the level—of house prices, and does this volatility amplification channel operate through tourism demand uncertainty or through investor composition shifts?
method: Panel GARCH-in-mean model estimated at the zip-code level with Airbnb listing density as an exogenous regressor of conditional variance, instrumenting listing density with city-level platform regulatory stringency interacted with pre-existing hotel supply, separately identified from the mean price-level effects already documented in the literature.
data: AirDNA short-term rental listing and revenue data (2014–2023) aggregated to zip code, Zillow Home Value Index and transaction-level data for volatility estimation, STR regulatory database from CBRE, and local hotel room supply from STR/CoStar.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in Airbnb listing density is expected to raise annualized zip-code-level house price volatility by 15–30%, over and above any mean price effect, with amplification strongest in resort and tourist-dependent markets; this implies that platforms impose an unpriced volatility externality that conventional price-level studies understate.
novelty_rationale: The corpus includes one paper on Airbnb bans and long-term rents but no paper models short-term rental platforms as a source of house price volatility rather than level, and none applies GARCH-in-mean architecture to connect platform economics with risk transmission in housing markets.
nn_title: Do short-term rental platforms affect housing markets? Evidence from Airbnb in B
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics, bubbles, and cycles

research_question: Can the revealed preferences of skilled workers—as measured by the residential location choices of H-1B visa holders around employer headquarters—be used to construct a revealed-preference amenity index that better predicts metropolitan house price growth than conventional hedonic amenity rankings?
method: Construct a skilled-worker revealed-preference amenity index from USCIS H-1B disclosure data matched to residential addresses via HMDA, then test its out-of-sample predictive power for 5- and 10-year MSA house price appreciation against standard quality-of-life indices using a horse-race predictive regression with expanding-window estimation.
data: USCIS H-1B employer location disclosure data (2010–2023), HMDA mortgage origination data for residential location proxies, Zillow/FHFA MSA-level house price indices, and BLS/Census data on standard amenity controls (crime, schools, climate).
key_finding: The H-1B revealed-preference index likely explains 20–35% of out-of-sample variance in 10-year MSA price appreciation beyond conventional amenity rankings, with metros ranking highly on skilled-worker preferences but poorly on traditional indices—suggesting that talent-location sorting is an under-utilized leading indicator of long-run land value formation.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive hedonic amenity capitalization literature and a few papers on innovation and housing prices, but no paper uses skilled-worker visa location choices as revealed-preference signals to build predictive housing market indices, importing revealed-preference methodology from urban labor economics into real estate forecasting.
nn_title: Warmth of the welcome: Immigration and local housing price dynamics
assigned_label: Hedonic Pricing Models and Housing Valuation

research_question: Do publicly traded REITs with properties in counties exposed to large-scale wildfire smoke events—measured by satellite-derived PM2.5 plumes rather than direct wildfire damage—experience persistent reductions in same-store NOI and occupancy distinct from physical destruction effects?
method: Satellite-derived daily county-level PM2.5 exposure data from NASA MERRA-2 is merged with REIT property-level NOI from SNL/CoStar supplemental filings, and a difference-in-differences design exploiting plausibly exogenous smoke plume trajectories driven by wind patterns identifies the causal effect of smoke exposure on operational performance and equity returns separately from fire damage.
data: NASA MERRA-2 aerosol optical depth reclassified to county-level PM2.5 (2010–2023), REIT property-level supplemental NOI and occupancy data from SNL Financial/S&P Global, CRSP daily stock returns, and NOAA wind trajectory data for instrument construction.
key_finding: Counties experiencing smoke events in the 90th percentile of annual PM2.5 exposure likely see 3–6% same-store NOI declines in office and multifamily REIT properties, with effects persisting 2–3 quarters, distinct from and additive to physical climate damage, implying that air-quality externalities from distant wildfires represent a systematically underpriced climate risk channel in REIT equity.
novelty_rationale: The corpus examines wildfires on mortgage pricing and REIT climate beta through asset damage, but no paper isolates the indirect economic channel of wildfire smoke—a geographically diffuse, wind-driven externality—on commercial real estate operations using satellite atmospheric data, which represents a genuinely new risk pathway.
nn_title: The Transmission of Climate Hazard Exposure to Equity Risk: Evidence From Real E
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: Does the staggered introduction of state-level digital notarization and remote online closing laws causally reduce mortgage origination costs, closing time, and racial gaps in mortgage access?
method: Difference-in-differences estimation exploiting the variation in adoption dates of Remote Online Notarization (RON) laws across U.S. states (2017–2023), using HMDA loan-level data to measure origination volumes, denial rates, and interest rate spreads, with triple-differences interacting adoption with borrower race and loan type to detect heterogeneous access effects.
data: HMDA nationwide loan-level origination data (2018–2023) covering 15+ million annual observations, Mortgage Bankers Association RON law adoption dates by state, CFPB mortgage cost survey data, and CoreLogic closing timeline data.
key_finding: States adopting RON laws likely see a 5–15% reduction in closing timelines and a 2–4% reduction in origination costs, with disproportionately larger access improvements for minority and rural borrowers who faced higher transaction-cost barriers, suggesting that digital closing infrastructure operates as a low-cost fair-lending intervention with heterogeneous distributional effects.
novelty_rationale: The corpus covers mortgage discrimination, broker roles, and information frictions extensively, but no paper exploits the quasi-experimental rollout of digital notarization laws as a regulatory technology shock that reduces transaction costs—a new institutional mechanism connecting mortgage technology policy to fair access questions.
nn_title: Differential Access to and Pricing of Home Mortgages: 2004 through 2009
assigned_label: Mortgage lending: discrimination, pricing, and risk

research_question: How do commercial real estate investors price the option value embedded in properties located in Special Economic Zones and tax increment financing districts when those districts approach their statutory expiration dates, and does expiration uncertainty generate predictable mispricing?
method: A real options framework is combined with a regression discontinuity design exploiting the discrete expiration dates of TIF districts, matching commercial properties inside expiring versus renewing TIF boundaries to estimate the market value of the fiscal incentive option and test whether cap rates adjust smoothly or exhibit the discrete discontinuities predicted by efficient markets.
data: Lincoln Institute of Land Policy TIF district boundary and expiration date database, CoStar commercial transaction and cap rate data (2000–2023) geocoded to TIF boundaries, MSCI/IPD property income data, and municipal fiscal records for district renewal decisions.
key_finding: Properties within TIF districts approaching expiration likely show 50–150 basis point cap rate increases relative to interior-district comparables, with evidence of systematic underpricing (too-slow adjustment) 12–18 months before expiration consistent with limited investor attention to tax-benefit optionality, creating predictable excess returns exploitable by informed investors.
novelty_rationale: The corpus includes a paper on TIF district formation effects but no paper models the expiration of fiscal incentive districts as a real option with testable pricing implications, combining real options theory—applied extensively to development timing in the corpus—with a novel institutional setting not previously examined.
nn_title: Demand Uncertainty, Development Timing and Leasehold Land Valuation: Empirical T
assigned_label: Real Estate Investment Timing and Options

research_question: Does neighborhood exposure to social media-amplified 'doom content' about crime and urban decay, measured by city-level crime-related TikTok and X (Twitter) post volumes, independently predict house price declines beyond actual recorded crime rates, and does this sentiment-price gap reverse when the social media narrative shifts?
method: City-week-level panel regressions of house price growth on social media crime sentiment indices constructed from publicly available TikTok video hashtag counts and X API post volumes, controlling for actual FBI UCR crime rates, local economic fundamentals, and using cross-platform sentiment disagreement as an instrument for pure narrative-driven price effects.
data: Publicly accessible TikTok hashtag volume data and X (Twitter) Academic API city-level post counts (2019–2023), FBI Uniform Crime Reporting data, Zillow city-level Home Value Index weekly observations, BLS local unemployment series, and Pew Research social media usage surveys for reach-weighting.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in crime-doom social media volume in a city predicts a 0.4–1.2% house price decline over the subsequent two quarters above and beyond actual crime movements, with reversion when narrative sentiment reverses, suggesting a new behavioral channel through which viral social media content generates transient but economically meaningful mispricings in urban housing markets.
novelty_rationale: The corpus includes papers on Google search indices, news media, and investor sentiment in housing and REIT markets, but no paper uses social video platform sentiment as a distinct channel connecting viral narrative economics to local residential prices, importing methods from computational social science into real estate economics.
nn_title: Quantifying Sentiment with News Media across Local Housing Markets
assigned_label: Real Estate Market Liquidity and Efficiency

research_question: Do public pension funds that have reduced their target allocations to private real estate following actuarial assumption resets experience measurable fire-sale discounts in their commercial property dispositions, and does this create predictable return opportunities for counter-cyclical buyers?
method: A matched-transaction design pairs pension fund dispositions—identified from Preqin LP commitment and disposition records linked to CoStar commercial sale transactions—with concurrent arm's-length market sales of comparable properties, using pension fund actuarial assumption change dates as instruments for forced-sale pressure to estimate fire-sale discounts and test for buyer return abnormalities.
data: Preqin private real estate fund LP and GP transaction database, Public Plans Data actuarial assumption records for 200+ U.S. public pension funds (2000–2023), CoStar commercial transaction data geocoded to seller type, and NCREIF index returns for benchmark-adjusted performance measurement.
key_finding: Pension fund-driven dispositions following actuarial assumption resets likely occur at 4–9% discounts to market comparables, with buyer abnormal returns of 150–300 basis points over the subsequent 3-year holding period, implying that institutional rebalancing constraints create persistent fire-sale externalities that well-capitalized counter-cyclical investors can systematically exploit.
novelty_rationale: The corpus covers institutional capital flows, REIT asset sales, and private real estate fund performance, but no paper exploits pension fund actuarial constraint changes as an exogenous shock to forced selling pressure in direct commercial real estate, connecting pension economics to the fire-sale pricing literature in a new institutional context.
nn_title: A Survey of Pension Fund Real Estate Portfolio Risk Management Practices
assigned_label: Real estate portfolio optimization and performance

research_question: Does the racial composition of a neighborhood's real estate agent workforce causally affect the racial gap in home sale prices achieved by minority sellers, beyond the effects of individual agent-client matching?
method: Shift-share instrumental variable design exploiting plausibly exogenous variation in local agent racial composition driven by historical immigration settlement patterns, combined with a hedonic repeat-sales framework that separates seller-side agent effects from buyer-side bargaining and neighborhood confounders.
data: HMDA loan-level data merged with NAR/state licensing board agent demographic records, CoreLogic deed transaction data with agent license identifiers (2000–2023), and 1980 Census immigrant settlement shares as instruments for current agent workforce composition across U.S. MSAs.
key_finding: Neighborhoods with higher shares of co-ethnic agents are expected to reduce the minority seller price penalty by 3–8 percentage points, operating primarily through reduced information asymmetry and stronger buyer networks rather than through direct negotiation skill differences.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus extensively studies racial disparities in mortgage pricing and appraisals, no existing work examines how the supply-side racial composition of the brokerage labor market itself causally shapes minority seller outcomes, opening a new supply-side discrimination channel.
nn_title: Ethnicity in housing markets: Buyers, sellers and agents
assigned_label: Racial Discrimination and Housing Market Prices

research_question: Does municipal broadband infrastructure deployment causally increase residential property values and alter the within-city spatial gradient of housing demand toward lower-density suburban and rural fringe locations?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting the quasi-random timing of municipal fiber-optic broadband rollout across U.S. counties and census tracts, with event-study plots and heterogeneity analysis by initial broadband penetration rates and pre-existing commuting patterns.
data: FCC Form 477 broadband availability data at the census block level (2010–2023), CoreLogic or Zillow transaction-level repeat-sales data, American Community Survey commuting flow data, and municipal broadband deployment dates from the Broadband Now and Institute for Local Self-Reliance municipal network databases.
key_finding: Municipal fiber deployment is expected to increase nearby residential values by 4–10 percent on average, with effects concentrated in previously underserved suburban fringe tracts and largest for single-family homes with dedicated office space, consistent with a remote-work amenity capitalization story.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains no study linking public telecommunications infrastructure investment to housing price gradients or the spatial restructuring of urban form, despite extensive work on transit capitalization and remote work trends, making this a genuinely underexplored public investment channel.
nn_title: Wireless Towers and Home Values: An Alternative Valuation Approach Using a Spati
assigned_label: Spatial econometrics in real estate valuation

research_question: Do real estate investors who experience large personal housing losses during local market downturns subsequently exhibit systematically more conservative leverage choices in their professional REIT portfolio management?
method: Linked individual-level analysis matching REIT executive residential transaction histories (from county deed records) to firm-level REIT leverage and acquisition activity, using the timing of personal home purchases relative to local house price collapses as an instrument for experienced loss severity, following the 'experience effects' literature in behavioral finance.
data: S&P Global Market Intelligence REIT financial panel data, county deed records from ATTOM Data Solutions matched to Execucomp and BoardEx executive biographical data, FHFA/CoreLogic MSA-level house price indices (1990–2022), and SEC Form 4 filings for executive residential address history.
key_finding: REIT managers who experienced larger personal home value losses during prior downturns are expected to maintain 5–15 percent lower leverage ratios in subsequent years and acquire fewer high-LTV properties, with effects persisting for 7–10 years and strongest for CEOs who became homeowners just before peak prices.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies CEO overconfidence and REIT capital structure separately but never imports the 'depression babies' experience-effects methodology from macrofinance to trace how personal real estate losses shape professional real estate investment decisions, constituting a novel bridge between behavioral economics and REIT governance.
nn_title: How do Personal Real Estate Transactions Affect Productivity and Risk Taking? Ev
assigned_label: REIT returns predictability and pricing dynamics

research_question: Does the mandatory disclosure of energy performance certificates at the point of sale generate persistent improvements in investment in building energy retrofits, or does disclosure without financial incentives produce only transitory capitalization effects that fade as salience decays?
method: Regression discontinuity design exploiting discrete rating thresholds within the EU Energy Performance Certificate framework combined with a difference-in-differences comparing pre- and post-mandatory disclosure periods across European jurisdictions that staggered implementation, with long-run follow-up on subsequent building permit applications for energy improvements.
data: UK Domestic Energy Performance Certificate register (20+ million certificates, 2008–2023) merged with Land Registry transaction data and local authority building permit records; analogous Dutch and Danish national EPC databases with permit records provide cross-country replication.
key_finding: Mandatory EPC disclosure is expected to generate a persistent 2–4 percent price premium at rating thresholds and a 15–25 percent increase in energy retrofit permit applications within two years, but the retrofit response is concentrated among owner-occupiers and absent among landlords, consistent with a split-incentive problem.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains several studies on energy label price effects but none uses the RD-at-threshold design to separately identify both the capitalization and the real investment response over long horizons, turning a static price study into a dynamic policy evaluation.
nn_title: Sustainable Building Certification and the Rent Premium: A Panel Data Approach
assigned_label: Green Building Features and Property Valuation

research_question: Do commercial real estate markets in cities with higher institutional investor common ownership of properties exhibit reduced rental competition and higher rent levels, analogous to the anticompetitive effects of common ownership documented in corporate equity markets?
method: Modified MHHI-delta measure of common ownership concentration at the MSA-property-type-quarter level computed from SNL/CoStar ownership records, estimated in a panel instrumental variables framework instrumenting common ownership with exogenous mergers between institutional investors' parent companies that alter portfolio overlap without changing local market fundamentals.
data: CoStar commercial lease transaction data (rents, vacancies) at the property level, SNL Financial and NCREIF institutional investor ownership records identifying investor-property linkages across U.S. MSAs (2000–2022), and institutional investor parent-company merger event data from SDC Platinum.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in common ownership concentration at the MSA-property-type level is expected to be associated with 3–7 percent higher effective rents and 2–4 percentage point higher vacancy rates, consistent with coordinated softening of rental competition rather than efficiency gains.
novelty_rationale: The common ownership anticompetitive effects literature from industrial organization (Azar et al.) has never been applied to real estate space markets; the corpus covers institutional ownership of REITs but not its competitive externalities on rental pricing in private markets, making this a genuinely new import from IO economics.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Do mortgage servicers strategically time loan modification offers to exploit borrowers' present bias, offering modifications immediately after distress events when borrowers are most likely to accept unfavorable terms?
method: Event-study hazard model using McDash/Black Knight loan-level servicing data that records exact dates of distress events (job loss, medical shock proxied by income drop) and modification offer timing, with a regression discontinuity at servicer-specific modification eligibility thresholds to separate strategic timing from borrower selection, augmented by a field experiment design using randomized modification offer timing in partnership with a servicer.
data: Black Knight McDash loan performance database with monthly servicer action records and modification terms (2008–2020), FEMA disaster declaration dates as exogenous income shock instruments, and CFPB complaint database for servicer-level variation in consumer responsiveness.
key_finding: Modification offers are expected to cluster within 30–60 days of distress onset when borrower discount rates are highest, with terms offered during this window 8–15 percent less favorable (lower payment reduction, shorter forbearance period) than offers made six months later, implying significant wealth transfer from distressed borrowers to servicers.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies modification determinants and servicer incentives extensively but never applies behavioral economics' present-bias framework to examine whether servicers strategically exploit the timing dimension of distress to extract surplus, representing an entirely new agency cost mechanism in mortgage servicing.
nn_title: Delinquencies, Default and Borrowers' Strategic Behavior toward the Modification
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Does the social connectedness of a neighborhood—measured by cross-neighborhood Facebook friendship links—predict the speed and magnitude of house price transmission across spatially distant but socially proximate communities?
method: Panel vector autoregression using the Facebook Social Connectedness Index (SCI) as a time-invariant weight matrix to construct social-network-based spatial lags of house price growth, with a horse-race against geographic distance, commuting flow, and demographic similarity weight matrices to identify the marginal informational content of social ties in house price diffusion.
data: Facebook Social Connectedness Index at the county-pair level (publicly released by Meta), Zillow/CoreLogic county-level quarterly house price indices (2005–2023), American Community Survey county-to-county commuting flows, and BLS county employment data as fundamentals controls.
key_finding: Social connectedness is expected to explain 15–25 percent of the residual cross-county house price co-movement unexplained by geographic proximity or commuting flows, with the social network channel strongest during boom periods when word-of-mouth search intensity is high and smallest during busts when fundamental selling pressure dominates.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies spatial house price diffusion using geographic and economic proximity but ignores social network topology; importing the Facebook SCI data—widely used in labor and public economics but absent from real estate research—opens a new information-channel explanation for the well-documented ripple effects.
nn_title: Local House Price Diffusion
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics, bubbles, and cycles

research_question: Do large-scale natural disasters that destroy housing stock in one metropolitan area generate detectable house price and rental inflation spillovers into geographically and socioeconomically connected receiving metros through displacement-driven demand shocks?
method: Synthetic difference-in-differences design treating each major disaster (Hurricanes Harvey, Irma, Maria, Ian; California wildfires) as a separate experiment, constructing synthetic control metros matched on pre-disaster housing market trends, with heterogeneity analysis by Social Connectedness Index strength between displaced origin and receiving counties as a measure of displacement probability.
data: FEMA National Flood Insurance Program claims and SBA disaster loan data for origin-county damage intensity, Zillow rent and listing price indices at the zip code level (2010–2023), IRS county-to-county migration flows, and FEMA Individual Assistance registrant destination addresses as direct displacement measures.
key_finding: Receiving metros connected to disaster-struck counties by strong migration and social ties are expected to experience 3–8 percent rent increases and 2–5 percent house price increases within 12 months of a major disaster, with effects dissipating over 18–24 months as housing supply adjusts, and concentrated in rental markets with low vacancy rates.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus studies local disaster effects on property values and 'distant hurricane' effects, no study formally quantifies the inter-metropolitan displacement externality using a matched synthetic control design with direct displacement data, which is essential for climate adaptation policy affecting both origin and receiving communities.
nn_title: Measuring Price Effects from Disasters Using Public Data: A Case Study of Hurric
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: Can textual sentiment extracted from central bank communications (Federal Reserve Beige Book regional real estate passages) predict commercial real estate transaction volume and cap rate movements 3–6 months ahead, beyond standard financial predictors?
method: Large-language-model sentiment scoring of the full corpus of Beige Book real estate passages (1970–2023) at the Federal Reserve district-quarter level, combined with panel predictive regressions of district-level CoStar transaction volume and NCREIF cap rates, with out-of-sample forecasting exercises and Granger causality tests controlling for interest rates, credit spreads, and GDP growth.
data: Federal Reserve Beige Book full text archives (publicly available from Federal Reserve websites, 1970–2023), CoStar commercial transaction volume and cap rate data by property type and Fed district, NCREIF Property Index quarterly returns, and St. Louis Fed FRED macro controls.
key_finding: Beige Book real estate sentiment is expected to predict commercial transaction volume growth with an R-squared increment of 8–15 percent over financial predictors alone, with the predictive content concentrated in the 1–2 quarter horizon and strongest for the office and retail sectors where central bank liaison contacts have superior local information.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains news-media sentiment studies for residential markets and REIT conference call analysis but never exploits the Beige Book—a uniquely systematic, high-frequency, geographically structured qualitative data source produced by the world's most influential monetary institution—for commercial real estate prediction.
nn_title: On the Relationship between Market Sentiment and Commercial Real Estate Performa
assigned_label: Real Estate Market Liquidity and Efficiency

research_question: Does the staggered adoption of inclusionary zoning ordinances across U.S. municipalities generate a detectable fiscal substitution effect, reducing local governments' investment in affordable housing subsidies while producing fewer below-market units than the nominal mandates imply due to developer opt-outs and reduced construction starts?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences with heterogeneity-robust estimators (Callaway-Sant'Anna) exploiting variation in the timing of inclusionary zoning adoption across California, Massachusetts, and New Jersey municipalities, with triple-difference analysis comparing municipalities with strong versus weak opt-out provisions and measuring outcomes on new permits, affordable unit delivery, and municipal affordable housing budget expenditures.
data: Furman Center/Grounded Solutions Network inclusionary zoning database of municipal ordinance adoption dates and features, U.S. Census Building Permits Survey at the place level (1990–2022), HUD CHAS affordable housing needs data, and municipal CAFR financial reports for affordable housing trust fund expenditures via the Lincoln Institute of Land Policy database.
key_finding: Inclusionary zoning adoption is expected to reduce market-rate construction starts by 8–15 percent in high-cost municipalities while delivering only 40–60 percent of mandated affordable units due to opt-outs, with municipalities simultaneously reducing dedicated affordable housing subsidy budgets by 15–25 percent, producing a net negative effect on total affordable housing supply.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies inclusionary housing stigma and production decisions theoretically but never empirically measures the fiscal substitution channel—the crowding-out of public affordable housing investment—using modern staggered DiD methods with comprehensive ordinance and municipal finance data, which is critical for evaluating the net policy impact.
nn_title: Guarding the Town Walls: Mechanisms and Motives for Restricting Multifamily Hous
assigned_label: Property taxation effects on housing markets

research_question: Does the geographic overlap between REIT property portfolios and federally designated Opportunity Zones predict differential capital expenditure responses and stock return performance relative to REITs with no Opportunity Zone exposure?
method: Difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered and geographically discontinuous designation of Opportunity Zones in 2018, using REIT property-level location data merged with OZ boundary shapefiles to construct firm-level OZ exposure shares, and estimating effects on capital expenditures, same-store NOI growth, and Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional returns.
data: NAREIT/SNL Real Estate property-level data for equity REITs (2015–2023), IRS Opportunity Zone census tract shapefiles, CRSP-Compustat merged database for REIT financials and stock returns, and NCREIF property-level capital expenditure records.
key_finding: REITs with high Opportunity Zone portfolio exposure are likely to exhibit elevated capital expenditures and positive abnormal returns in the years immediately following designation, with effects concentrated in multifamily and industrial property types that most closely match OZ investment incentive structures.
novelty_rationale: While prior work examines OZ effects on commercial property prices and residential rents, no study has examined how OZ geography shapes the internal capital allocation and equity pricing of publicly traded REITs, connecting the OZ literature to the REIT capital budgeting and local market risk literatures simultaneously.
nn_title: Geographic Portfolio Allocations, Property Selection and Performance Attribution
assigned_label: Real estate portfolio diversification and geographic factors

research_question: Does air traffic noise exposure—identified through exogenous variation in flight path rerouting following FAA NextGen implementation—causally affect the spatial sorting of high-income households and the capitalization of school quality premiums into nearby residential prices?
method: Regression discontinuity in geographic space exploiting the precise boundaries of new flight paths introduced by FAA NextGen reforms between 2012 and 2019, combined with a heterogeneous treatment effects hedonic model that interacts noise exposure with school quality rankings to test whether school premiums are crowded out by noise disamenity.
data: FAA aircraft track data and NextGen implementation schedules (publicly available via FAA ADS-B data), CoreLogic deed-level transaction records, American Community Survey income and demographic data, state school report card data, and EPA noise contour maps.
key_finding: Neighborhoods newly placed under NextGen flight paths experience significant price declines concentrated in school-premium zones, with the school quality capitalization premium shrinking by 20–40% in areas subject to large noise increases, indicating that environmental disamenity crowds out valuation of local public goods.
novelty_rationale: The literature treats school quality and environmental disamenity capitalization as parallel but separate topics; this study is the first to test whether noise shocks erode the mechanism through which households bid up prices for school access, bridging the hedonic disamenity and school capitalization literatures using a credible quasi-experiment.
nn_title: Continuation of air services at Berlin‐Tegel and its effects on apartment rental
assigned_label: School Quality Capitalization in Housing Markets

research_question: Do hospital system mergers—which alter the geographic footprint of major employment anchors—cause measurable ripple effects in surrounding commercial office and medical office real estate rents and vacancy rates?
method: Event study combined with difference-in-differences using hospital merger announcement and completion dates from the American Hospital Association Annual Survey as treatment events, comparing office rent and vacancy trajectories in ZIP codes containing merging hospitals versus matched control ZIP codes without merger activity, identified via propensity score matching on pre-merger market characteristics.
data: American Hospital Association Annual Survey (1998–2022) for merger events and hospital location, CoStar Group commercial lease transaction data for medical and general office properties, Bureau of Labor Statistics Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for local labor market controls, and Census ZIP code business patterns.
key_finding: Hospital mergers that result in facility consolidation generate significant vacancy increases (5–12 percentage points) and rent declines in the vicinity of closed campuses, with positive spillovers to surviving campus neighborhoods, demonstrating that anchor employer restructuring is a first-order driver of local commercial real estate market dynamics.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive work on agglomeration and anchor effects in retail and office markets but no study connects hospital industry consolidation—the largest employer restructuring trend in the U.S. services sector—to commercial real estate outcomes, importing insights from health economics into property market research.
nn_title: Local and National Determinants of Office Vacancies
assigned_label: Commercial Office Market Dynamics and Pricing

research_question: Can the cross-sectional dispersion of internet search intensity for specific housing submarkets, measured through Google Trends data, predict subsequent metropolitan-level house price volatility and transaction volume collapse with greater lead time than conventional survey-based expectations measures?
method: Panel vector autoregression estimated on monthly metropolitan-level data, where the treatment variable is a novel Google Trends composite index constructed from housing-distress search terms (e.g., 'foreclosure help,' 'sell my house fast,' 'housing crash') disaggregated to the MSA level, benchmarked against the Michigan Survey of Consumer Expectations and Fannie Mae Home Purchase Sentiment Index in out-of-sample forecasting contests.
data: Google Trends API data at the DMA/MSA level (2004–2024), Zillow Research house price and inventory data at the metro level, NAR existing home sales data, Fannie Mae Home Purchase Sentiment Index, University of Michigan Survey of Consumers, and CoreLogic distress metrics.
key_finding: Housing-distress search intensity leads conventional survey expectations by two to four months in predicting price deceleration and volume contraction, with the predictive advantage most pronounced during transitional market phases, suggesting search behavior reveals private household information not captured by stated surveys.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus contains work on internet search and housing markets, no study directly competes distress-specific search composites against established institutional sentiment indices in a forecasting horse race at the metro level with a structural identification of information timing.
nn_title: Google Search Queries, Foreclosures, and House Prices
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does the staggered enactment of state eviction expungement laws—which seal or destroy court records of prior eviction filings—causally improve renter access to higher-quality housing and reduce residential segregation by income?
method: Stacked difference-in-differences exploiting the timing variation in eviction record expungement legislation across 12 U.S. states that enacted such laws between 2015 and 2023, using Princeton Eviction Lab court record data to measure eviction filing rates, American Community Survey microdata to measure residential mobility and housing quality transitions among renter households, and Zillow observed rental price data to assess landlord supply-side responses.
data: Princeton Eviction Lab county-level eviction filing and judgment data (2000–2023), IPUMS American Community Survey microdata (annual 2010–2023), Zillow Observed Rent Index at the ZIP code level, state legislative records for eviction expungement law dates from the National Housing Law Project, and HUD Fair Market Rents.
