key_finding: States that enact expungement laws experience measurable improvements in renter residential mobility toward higher-quality ZIP codes and modest reductions in income-based residential segregation, though potential landlord screening adjustments may partially offset these benefits in tight rental markets.
novelty_rationale: No existing real estate economics study has examined how eviction record expungement laws reshape rental housing market equilibria, tenant sorting, and segregation outcomes, despite this being one of the most active areas of housing policy reform, creating an entirely new empirical frontier in tenant protection research.
nn_title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evi
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Do CEOs of publicly traded REITs who simultaneously hold personal residential real estate in geographic markets where their firm invests exhibit systematically different acquisition pricing and return outcomes compared to CEOs without such local residential ties?
method: Matching personal residential property records of REIT CEOs—obtained from county assessor deed databases and linked to Execucomp CEO identifiers—to REIT property acquisition locations, then estimating a hedonic acquisition price model that controls for property characteristics and tests whether CEO residential overlap predicts overpayment or superior information-driven pricing, with instrumental variables using CEO residential history before firm appointment.
data: NAREIT/SNL Real Estate REIT property acquisition transaction data, Execucomp CEO identification and tenure data, county assessor deed records for residential properties (available from ATTOM Data Solutions or CoreLogic public records), Compustat for REIT financials, and CRSP for stock return outcomes.
key_finding: CEOs with personal residential holdings in the same submarkets as firm acquisitions pay lower acquisition prices and generate higher subsequent property-level returns, consistent with local information advantages dominating agency concerns, but only in markets with low analyst coverage where information asymmetry is high.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive work on REIT CEO compensation, overconfidence, and agency costs, but no study has used personal residential property holdings as a revealed-preference measure of CEO geographic information endowment, bridging the insider trading, local information, and REIT governance literatures in a novel way.
nn_title: CEO–CFO team optimism: Commercial real estate transactions and REIT performance
assigned_label: Corporate Governance and REIT Firm Value

research_question: Does the racial composition of mortgage servicer workforces—identified through EEO-1 employer reports—predict differential loss mitigation offer rates and foreclosure timelines for minority versus white borrowers conditional on identical delinquency status?
method: Two-stage empirical design linking EEO-1 aggregate workforce diversity data for the largest mortgage servicers to loan-level performance outcomes in the CFPB HMDA enhanced dataset and McDash Analytics mortgage performance records, exploiting within-servicer variation in workforce diversity changes over time and cross-servicer variation via servicer transfer events as quasi-experiments.
data: CFPB HMDA loan-level data (2018–2023) with servicer identifiers, McDash Analytics or Black Knight mortgage performance data for delinquency and modification outcomes, EEO-1 Component 1 data (publicly released aggregated workforce diversity by employer and occupation for employers with 100+ employees), and FDIC servicer identification crosswalks.
key_finding: Servicers with higher shares of minority employees in loan servicing occupations exhibit smaller racial gaps in loss mitigation offer rates and shorter time-to-modification for minority borrowers, with effects concentrated among Spanish-speaking Hispanic borrowers where language concordance is most relevant.
novelty_rationale: The corpus extensively examines racial disparities in mortgage lending origination and pricing, but no study has examined the servicer workforce diversity channel in loss mitigation outcomes, importing organizational demography methods from the management literature into mortgage market discrimination research.
nn_title: The Impact of Minority Representation at Mortgage Lenders
assigned_label: Mortgage lending: discrimination, pricing, and risk

research_question: How does the rollout of 5G small cell infrastructure across urban street-by-street deployment schedules affect commercial real estate rents and property values in treated versus untreated blocks, and does this effect differ between data-intensive tenants (tech, finance) and foot-traffic-dependent retailers?
method: Street-level difference-in-differences exploiting FCC-registered small cell installation records as treatment timing, matched to CoStar lease transaction data and Lightbox property assessment records at the parcel level, with heterogeneity analysis by NAICS tenant code to distinguish effects across retail, office, and industrial property types within the same building or block.
data: FCC Universal Licensing System small cell registration data with GPS coordinates and installation dates (publicly available), CoStar commercial lease transaction records including asking and effective rents by tenant NAICS code, Lightbox/CoreLogic parcel-level property assessment and transaction data, and FCC broadband deployment maps.
key_finding: 5G small cell installation generates significant rent premiums (3–8%) in office and industrial properties occupied by data-intensive tenants, while retail properties on treated blocks show neutral to negative effects due to reduced dwell-time incentives for pedestrian shoppers, with the industrial effect largest in last-mile logistics corridors.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus contains work on transportation and utility infrastructure effects on property values, no study has estimated the commercial real estate value implications of 5G small cell deployment using granular FCC registration data with tenant-type heterogeneity, despite 5G representing the largest ongoing urban infrastructure transformation.
nn_title: Wireless Towers and Home Values: An Alternative Valuation Approach Using a Spati
assigned_label: Transportation Infrastructure Impact on Property Values

research_question: Does the revelation of persistent hidden water quality failures—identified through EPA Safe Drinking Water Act violation records—cause persistent capitalization effects in residential housing markets, and do these effects differ by household income and racial composition of neighborhoods through differential information access?
method: Event study with difference-in-differences using the timing of EPA public water system violation disclosures as treatment events, exploiting variation in violation severity, duration of concealment, and media coverage intensity to test whether information salience moderates price effects, with heterogeneous treatment effects estimated across neighborhood income and racial composition terciles using CoreLogic deed data.
data: EPA Safe Drinking Water Information System (SDWIS) violation records with public water system service area boundaries (publicly available), CoreLogic deed-level residential transaction data, U.S. Census American Community Survey neighborhood demographic and income data, NewsAPI or ProQuest news coverage data for media salience measurement, and EPA Environmental Justice screening tool data.
key_finding: Water quality violations that receive high media coverage cause price declines of 4–9% concentrated near violation-period transactions, but low-income and minority neighborhoods show systematically smaller price responses despite greater health burden, consistent with differential information access and lower household search capacity amplifying environmental justice disparities.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains hedonic studies of environmental contamination but none that exploit the differential information revelation mechanism across neighborhood demographic contexts using a nationally comprehensive violation database, connecting the environmental justice and hedonic information literatures in a novel empirical framework.
nn_title: House prices and septic tanks: Evidence from Miami‐Dade county
assigned_label: Environmental Contamination and Property Values

research_question: Do institutional landlords—specifically single-family rental corporations with large geographically concentrated portfolios—respond to local housing supply shocks differently than small landlords in terms of rent-setting, maintenance investment, and lease renewal decisions, and do these behavioral differences amplify or dampen local rental market volatility?
method: Exploiting the staggered geographic expansion of publicly traded SFR REITs (Invitation Homes, AMH, Tricon) as an instrument for institutional landlord market share using HMDA deed and county assessor ownership records, with outcome variables including Zillow observed rents at the ZIP level, American Housing Survey maintenance expenditure self-reports, and eviction filing rates from Princeton Eviction Lab, estimated via a generalized synthetic control approach.
data: County assessor deed records with corporate buyer identification (ATTOM or CoreLogic), Princeton Eviction Lab eviction filing data, Zillow Observed Rent Index at ZIP code level, HMDA deed records with investor flag, American Housing Survey unit-level maintenance data, SEC 10-K property-level acquisition disclosures for public SFR REITs, and CoStar/RealPage rental market data.
key_finding: High institutional SFR market share is associated with greater rent rigidity downward during demand contractions but faster rent increases during supply shortfalls, with reduced eviction filing rates but lower maintenance investment relative to mom-and-pop landlords, indicating that portfolio optimization by institutional investors amplifies rental market procyclicality.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus contains work on institutional investors and single-family housing prices, no study has jointly examined the rent-setting, maintenance, and eviction behaviors of institutionally owned SFR portfolios as a system using multiple administrative data sources, connecting REIT operational behavior to rental market dynamics at the neighborhood level.
nn_title: The rise of corporate landlords: An examination of behavioral differences in the
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Does the staggered legalization of recreational cannabis across U.S. states causally alter the spatial distribution of commercial real estate cap rates, rents, and vacancy rates for retail and industrial properties, with effects concentrated in municipalities that permit dispensaries and cultivation facilities?
method: Stacked difference-in-differences design exploiting the staggered timing of state recreational cannabis legalization and subsequent municipal opt-in decisions as quasi-random treatment variation, with event-study plots for pre-trend validation and heterogeneity analysis by property type and local zoning stringency.
data: CoStar commercial property transaction and lease data (2010–2024) merged with state and municipal cannabis legalization dates from the National Conference of State Legislatures, dispensary location data from Leafly's public API, and local zoning records from municipal open-data portals.
key_finding: Retail properties within a quarter-mile of licensed dispensaries are expected to show rent premiums of 5–15% and lower vacancy rates due to foot-traffic spillovers, while industrial properties in cultivation-permissive jurisdictions show cap rate compression reflecting new tenant demand, with effects reversed in opt-out municipalities.
novelty_rationale: No existing REE or JREFE paper examines how cannabis legalization reshapes commercial real estate markets at the property level; the industrial-cultivation channel is entirely unexplored and the stacked DiD design imports modern econometric advances not yet standard in the commercial real estate literature.
nn_title: Getting high or getting low? the external effects of coffeeshops on house prices
assigned_label: Crime's Effects on Real Estate Markets

research_question: Do financially distressed households strategically time the sale of their primary residence to coincide with peaks in local housing market liquidity, and does this timing capacity vary systematically with homeowner financial sophistication as proxied by credit score trajectory and prior investment account ownership?
method: Hazard model of time-to-sale with competing risks (voluntary sale versus foreclosure) estimated on matched mortgage servicing and credit bureau panel data, instrumenting for market liquidity timing using idiosyncratic variation in local listing inventory driven by seasonal weather shocks orthogonal to borrower distress.
data: Black Knight McDash mortgage performance data linked to Equifax or TransUnion credit bureau records (available through Federal Reserve Bank research programs), merged with Zillow Research's monthly metro-level listing inventory and days-on-market series (publicly available).
key_finding: Homeowners with higher financial sophistication are expected to time sales 30–90 days earlier in the liquidity cycle, reducing losses by 5–12% compared to unsophisticated distressed sellers, with the gap widening in markets with greater seasonality and closing in states with longer foreclosure timelines that provide more decision time.
novelty_rationale: The corpus extensively studies foreclosure and distress outcomes but treats seller timing as exogenous; importing behavioral finance insights on financial sophistication into a competing-risks framework that jointly models strategic timing and financial distress represents a genuinely new intersection of mortgage, behavioral, and search literatures.
nn_title: Foreclosure Externalities and Home Liquidity
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Does the algorithmic co-location of short-term rental listings by platform recommendation engines create spatial clustering of Airbnb supply that exacerbates housing affordability externalities beyond what individual host decisions would produce, and do these platform-induced clusters differ in their rent spillover effects from organically dispersed listings?
method: Spatial regression discontinuity exploiting abrupt changes in platform-recommended neighborhood boundaries—identified through systematic scraping of Airbnb's neighborhood guide pages at multiple time points—combined with a two-stage instrumental variables estimator using platform recommendation indicators to instrument for listing density in boundary-adjacent census tracts.
data: Inside Airbnb's publicly archived city-level listing snapshots (covering 100+ U.S. and international cities, 2015–2024) merged with Census Bureau American Community Survey rental vacancy and median rent data, with platform neighborhood boundary coordinates digitized from archived Airbnb neighborhood guide pages via the Wayback Machine.
key_finding: Platform-recommended cluster zones are expected to show 8–20% larger negative rent spillovers on long-term rental markets compared to equally dense but organically dispersed listings, because clustering concentrates demand shocks and reduces landlord search frictions for conversion, with effects strongest in cities with low rental vacancy rates.
novelty_rationale: Existing short-term rental research treats platform listings as exogenous individual decisions; this paper introduces platform algorithmic design as a structural determinant of housing externalities, importing industrial organization insights about recommendation systems into housing economics in a way entirely absent from the corpus.
nn_title: Short-term rentals and the housing market: Quasi-experimental evidence from Airb
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Does exposure to the January effect in publicly traded REITs transmit to private commercial real estate valuations through the appraisal updating behavior of institutional investors who hold both asset classes, creating a calendar-induced bias in appraisal-based return indices?
method: Panel regression of appraisal revision magnitudes on REIT return shocks in the prior calendar month, with treatment intensity varying by the share of institutional investors who simultaneously hold public REITs and private real estate (identified from 13F filings and NCREIF member records), controlling for economic fundamentals and property fixed effects.
data: NCREIF Property Index quarterly appraisal-level data (available to academic researchers through NCREIF membership or research agreements), SEC 13F institutional holdings data (publicly available through EDGAR), and CRSP/Bloomberg daily REIT return data, covering 1994–2023.
key_finding: Appraisals updated in January–February are expected to show 15–30 basis point upward revisions correlated with prior-month REIT performance, with the effect strongest for property types with close REIT analogs (apartments, industrial) and for institutions with the highest dual-market exposure, implying that private market return indices inherit a spurious seasonal component.
novelty_rationale: The corpus separately studies REIT calendar anomalies and appraisal smoothing but never links the two through an investor-behavior channel; this paper provides a micro-founded mechanism for why private market indices may inherit public market noise, opening a new research program at the intersection of market integration and appraisal methodology.
nn_title: Changes in REIT Structure and Stock Performance: Evidence from the Monday Stock
assigned_label: REIT returns predictability and pricing dynamics

research_question: Do mandatory seismic retrofit ordinances for soft-story buildings—which create large, unexpected capital expenditure obligations—cause differential house price effects by owner type, with landlord-owned buildings experiencing larger discounts than owner-occupied ones due to rent control constraints that prevent cost pass-through?
method: Difference-in-differences with a geographic regression discontinuity at the soft-story building zone boundary, comparing properties subject to mandatory retrofit orders with observationally similar properties just outside the zone, interacted with rental versus owner-occupancy status and local rent control intensity, using the staggered rollout of retrofit ordinances across California municipalities as additional identifying variation.
data: Los Angeles and San Francisco building permit records (publicly available through city open-data portals) identifying soft-story buildings and retrofit completion dates, linked to CoreLogic or Zillow transaction data, combined with rent control jurisdiction maps from the National Multifamily Housing Council and Census Bureau ACS owner-occupancy rates by building.
key_finding: Rental properties subject to retrofit mandates in rent-controlled jurisdictions are expected to sell at 8–18% discounts relative to owner-occupied analogs, because landlords cannot recapture retrofit costs through rent increases; the discount is expected to narrow after retrofit completion as the liability is resolved, and to be absent in jurisdictions without rent control.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies environmental liabilities and rent control separately but never examines their interaction through mandatory capital expenditure shocks; this design provides a clean quasi-experiment linking building regulation, rent control, and property valuation through the under-studied channel of compliance cost capitalization across tenure types.
nn_title: Do Tenants Capture the Benefits from the Low‐Income Housing Tax Credit Program?
assigned_label: Real Estate Finance Research Frontiers

research_question: Do corporate bankruptcy filings by major anchor tenants produce spatially differentiated rent and vacancy spillovers across shopping center trade areas, with effects decaying nonlinearly with distance and moderated by the tenant mix diversity of neighboring retail centers?
method: Event study combined with spatial econometric analysis, using Chapter 11 and Chapter 7 filings by major retailers (Sears, JCPenney, Pier 1, Bed Bath & Beyond) as quasi-exogenous shocks and measuring rent and vacancy outcomes for CoStar-tracked properties within concentric distance rings, with spillover decay estimated using a spatial Durbin model that allows for heterogeneous effects by center quality tier.
data: CoStar retail property lease and vacancy panel data (2005–2023) merged with PACER federal bankruptcy filing records identifying store closure dates and locations, combined with ICSC shopping center registry data on anchor tenant identity and center classification by gross leasable area.
key_finding: Retail properties within one mile of a major anchor bankruptcy are expected to experience 4–9% rent declines and 3–6 percentage point vacancy rate increases, with effects concentrated in lower-quality strip centers and attenuating for regional malls that benefit from re-leasing activity; tenant mix entropy at neighboring centers is expected to reduce contagion by 30–50%.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus studies tenant concentration and retail cap rates, no paper systematically quantifies the spatial contagion from anchor bankruptcy shocks across competing centers using modern spatial econometrics, representing a missing piece in the literature on inter-store externalities and shopping center dynamics with direct implications for retail CMBS risk.
nn_title: Retail Concentration and Shopping Center Rents—A Comparison of Two Cities
assigned_label: Shopping center economics and retail performance

research_question: Does the genetic component of time preference—identified through polygenic scores for patience and impulsivity derived from genome-wide association studies—explain residual variation in mortgage leverage choices, refinancing timing, and default propensity after controlling for all observable financial and demographic characteristics?
method: Two-sample Mendelian randomization using published polygenic scores for economic time preferences (from GWAS studies) as instruments for impatience, linked to mortgage origination and performance records through datasets that jointly observe genetic data and financial outcomes, with robustness checks using the MR-Egger and weighted median estimators to control for pleiotropic pathways.
data: Health and Retirement Study (HRS) which contains both genetic polygenic scores and detailed mortgage holding, refinancing, and wealth data for older U.S. households, merged with geocoded CoreLogic mortgage performance data for validation of geographic default patterns; HRS data available through restricted-use agreements with University of Michigan.
key_finding: A one standard deviation increase in the polygenic score for patience is expected to reduce mortgage default probability by 8–15% and increase refinancing timeliness by 20–30 days, with effects concentrated among liquidity-constrained borrowers where behavioral factors have the most decision-making scope, and among cohorts who obtained mortgages before widespread financial counseling.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains one twin study on homeownership genetics but no paper applies modern polygenic score methods or Mendelian randomization to mortgage behavior; importing this genomic epidemiology toolkit into mortgage economics is a genuine methodological frontier that could disentangle preference heterogeneity from selection in default models.
nn_title: A Search for the Genetic Contributors to Strategic Mortgage Default: The Catecho
assigned_label: Mortgage behavior, defaults, and termination risks

research_question: Does the adoption of crime prediction algorithms (predictive policing software) by municipal police departments alter residential property values differentially by neighborhood racial composition, and do these capitalization effects reflect increased perceived safety or heightened surveillance disamenity depending on local context?
method: Stacked difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered adoption of PredPol, ShotSpotter, and similar predictive policing systems across U.S. cities (identified from ACLU public records requests and investigative journalism databases), with heterogeneous treatment effects estimated by tract-level racial composition and crime rate pre-trends, using repeat-sales indices to control for time-invariant property characteristics.
data: Zillow Research transaction-level repeat-sales data merged with ACLU-compiled predictive policing adoption records (available through ACLU state affiliate reports and Stanford Open Policing Project), combined with FBI Uniform Crime Reporting data for pre-trend controls and Census Bureau racial composition from ACS five-year estimates.
key_finding: Adoption of predictive policing is expected to increase property values by 2–5% in predominantly white, low-crime neighborhoods (safety amenity effect) but decrease values by 3–8% in predominantly minority, high-crime neighborhoods (surveillance disamenity and over-policing effect), with divergence widening over time as system use intensifies, implying algorithmic policing amplifies existing spatial inequality in housing markets.
novelty_rationale: The corpus extensively studies crime-housing price relationships but treats policing as exogenous; this is the first design to isolate algorithmic law enforcement technology as a distinct treatment in housing markets, importing computational social science and civil liberties literatures into real estate economics to study a policy question of growing societal importance.
nn_title: Crime risk and housing values: Evidence from the gun offender registry
assigned_label: Crime's Effects on Real Estate Markets

research_question: Do mortgage lenders systematically underprice flood risk in origination decisions when the underlying property is located in a congressional district represented by a legislator who sits on the House Financial Services or Senate Banking Committee, reflecting political regulatory forbearance?
method: Regression discontinuity in close congressional elections that change committee membership, comparing mortgage origination volumes, LTV ratios, and flood insurance requirements for properties in affected districts before and after close election outcomes, with a difference-in-differences overlay using FEMA special flood hazard area boundaries as a continuous risk measure to separate political effects from geographic sorting.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) loan-level origination data (publicly available through CFPB), merged with FEMA National Flood Insurance Program flood zone maps and claims data (publicly available), Congressional committee membership records from the Congressional Research Service, and MIT Election Data Science Lab electoral margin data.
key_finding: Mortgages originated in districts whose representatives gain Banking Committee seats are expected to show 5–10% higher origination volumes in high-risk flood zones, 3–6 percentage point lower flood insurance compliance rates, and higher subsequent delinquency rates during flood events, consistent with regulatory capture reducing underwriting discipline in politically connected districts.
novelty_rationale: The corpus studies political risk in real estate and flood risk in mortgages as separate streams; no paper links Congressional committee power to mortgage underwriting standards through a political economy channel, representing a genuinely new intersection of political science, regulatory economics, and mortgage finance with direct implications for climate risk policy.
nn_title: Who Bears Flood Risk? Evidence from Mortgage Markets in Florida
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: Does the staggered rollout of automated license plate reader (ALPR) networks across U.S. cities produce capitalization effects in commercial real estate that differ by property type, with parking-intensive retail and office properties losing value due to reduced consumer search frictions reducing captive demand while reducing crime premiums benefit all classes?
method: Difference-in-differences with event-study design exploiting the staggered city-level and precinct-level deployment of ALPR networks (identified from police department FOIA releases and vendor public announcements), with heterogeneous effects estimated by property-level parking space count from CoStar, distance to parking garages, and proximity to prior auto-theft hotspots identified from geocoded crime data.
data: CoStar commercial property transaction, rent, and vacancy data merged with ALPR deployment records assembled from ACLU's Surveillance Technology Oversight Project and EFF's Atlas of Surveillance (both publicly available), combined with FBI NIBRS geocoded crime data and CoStar's parking space count field for commercial properties.
key_finding: ALPR deployment is expected to increase property values for all commercial types by 1–3% through crime reduction, but parking-intensive retail properties near high-turnover parking facilities experience an additional 2–4% discount reflecting reduced parking scarcity rents and consumer dwell time, with the net effect negative for surface-lot-dependent strip retail in auto-oriented markets.
novelty_rationale: No paper in the corpus studies surveillance infrastructure as a commercial real estate externality; this design bridges urban economics, criminology, and transportation planning to identify a previously unmeasured channel through which digital infrastructure reshapes commercial property values—the parking scarcity mechanism is particularly novel and counter-intuitive.
nn_title: The Grass Isn’t Always Greener: How Crime Influences Property Values Near Urban
assigned_label: Crime's Effects on Real Estate Markets

research_question: Do large-scale renewable energy permitting decisions—specifically the approval or rejection of utility-scale solar and wind projects on federal and state lands—generate measurable capitalization effects in nearby rural and exurban housing markets through anticipated land use change and visual externalities?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting quasi-random variation in project approval timing from NEPA environmental review decisions, combined with hedonic pricing models using parcel-level transaction data and viewshed analysis derived from digital elevation models to distinguish visual exposure from proximity effects.
data: BLM and FERC project approval records (2005–2023), CoreLogic parcel-level transaction data for rural counties in the Western U.S., USGS digital elevation models for viewshed computation, and county assessor records for property characteristics.
key_finding: Properties within visible distance of approved utility-scale renewable installations are expected to experience 3–12% price declines during the approval-to-construction phase, with heterogeneous recovery depending on whether turbine/panel density and local attitudes toward clean energy moderate the stigma effect.
novelty_rationale: While wind turbine externalities have been studied locally, no study combines quasi-experimental identification from federal permitting timing with viewshed-based spatial exposure measures and the full renewable energy portfolio (solar and wind) across a national panel, nor connects these to the literature on energy transition risk pricing in real assets.
nn_title: Wind Energy Facilities and Residential Properties: The Effect of Proximity and V
assigned_label: Environmental Contamination and Property Values

research_question: Does the mandatory public disclosure of corporate carbon emissions under SEC climate risk rules and voluntary CDP reporting alter capitalization rates and transaction prices for commercial real estate assets owned by high-emitting firms, creating a brown discount in the direct property market that complements the green premium literature?
method: Matched-sample difference-in-differences using staggered CDP disclosure events as a quasi-experiment, comparing transaction-based cap rates and prices for properties owned by disclosing versus non-disclosing firms before and after first disclosure, instrumented by index-driven ownership changes that force passive disclosure.
data: CoStar commercial transaction database (2010–2023), CDP corporate emissions disclosure records, MSCI RCA transaction-level cap rate data, S&P 500 index constituent lists for instrument construction, and GRESB sustainability scores for robustness.
key_finding: Properties owned by firms disclosing above-median Scope 1 and 2 emissions are expected to trade at cap rate spreads 25–75 basis points wider than matched peers, with the discount amplifying post-2020 as ESG investor mandates intensify.
novelty_rationale: The existing green buildings literature focuses on building-level energy certification; this paper uniquely traces corporate-level climate disclosure to direct property market pricing via ownership linkage, introducing the 'brown owner discount' as a distinct mechanism from physical asset efficiency effects.
nn_title: The Transmission of Climate Hazard Exposure to Equity Risk: Evidence From Real E
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does the staggered adoption of state-level algorithmic rent-setting regulations—which restrict or mandate disclosure of AI-driven pricing tools used by large residential landlords—causally affect rent levels, lease renewal rates, and vacancy dynamics in affected multifamily markets?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered passage of state laws restricting algorithmic pricing software (e.g., RealPage) from 2023 onward, comparing unit-level rents and vacancy rates in treated versus untreated MSAs using a synthetic control approach to account for pre-treatment trend heterogeneity.
data: CoStar and Yardi Matrix unit-level rent and occupancy data for multifamily properties (2018–2024), state legislative records and enactment dates for algorithmic pricing restrictions, and census-tract demographic and housing supply elasticity controls.
key_finding: Regulation-induced removal of coordinated algorithmic pricing is expected to reduce effective rents by 2–8% in high-landlord-concentration submarkets, with larger effects in markets where the top three institutional owners controlled more than 20% of Class A supply.
novelty_rationale: No existing real estate economics paper causally identifies the effect of algorithmic rent coordination restrictions on market outcomes; this imports the industrial organization literature on platform competition and collusion into the housing market, addressing a regulatory frontier with immediate policy relevance.
nn_title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evi
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Can neuroscientific measures of salience and loss aversion—elicited through large-scale online choice experiments linked to actual housing transaction histories—explain which property characteristics generate anchoring bias in seller reservation prices beyond what standard prospect theory predicts?
method: A two-stage design combining a pre-registered incentive-compatible online choice experiment (using Amazon Mechanical Turk linked to participants' self-reported transaction histories) that elicits salience weights for housing attributes, followed by structural estimation of a search model where anchoring magnitude is parameterized by experimentally derived salience scores and validated against administrative housing transaction data.
data: Amazon Mechanical Turk participant pool (minimum n=5,000) linked to self-reported home purchase/sale histories verified against Zillow/Trulia public listing archives, CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction data for validation, and ACS housing stock characteristics for local market controls.
key_finding: Attributes with high perceptual salience (bedrooms, listed square footage) are expected to generate anchoring coefficients 30–50% larger than low-salience features (lot size, age), explaining cross-sectional heterogeneity in price-to-list ratios that standard loss-aversion models cannot account for.
novelty_rationale: While anchoring in housing markets has been documented, no study connects neuroscientifically validated salience measures from experimental economics to the structural estimation of seller price-setting; this bridges cognitive neuroscience, experimental economics, and real estate search theory.
nn_title: Loss Aversion and Anchoring in Commercial Real Estate Pricing: Empirical Evidenc
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does the geographic expansion of large-format fulfillment center networks by e-commerce platforms create measurable wage spillovers in surrounding labor markets, and do these wage spillovers subsequently capitalize into residential property values with a lag that reveals the speed of household location adjustment?
method: A two-stage instrumental variables approach using the predicted placement of fulfillment centers from a least-cost routing algorithm based on pre-existing highway network topology as an instrument, estimating first-stage wage effects using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data, then regressing parcel-level transaction prices on predicted wage residuals within 15-mile rings using a dynamic distributed lag panel model.
data: Commercially available fulfillment center location database (MWPVL International, 2010–2023), BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages at the zip-code level, CoreLogic parcel transaction data, and TIGER/Line highway shapefiles for instrument construction.
key_finding: A 10% predicted wage increase from fulfillment center entry is expected to raise surrounding residential prices by 3–6% over a 3–5 year adjustment period, with faster capitalization in supply-inelastic markets and larger effects for entry-level housing than luxury segments.
novelty_rationale: While Amazon HQ2 effects have been studied and individual fulfillment center papers exist, no study uses the full national network rollout to identify the wage-to-house-price transmission channel with a quasi-experimental instrument, nor estimates the dynamic adjustment speed as a testable implication of household location theory.
nn_title: Spatial Versus Non-Spatial Determinants of Shopping Center Rents: Modeling Locat
assigned_label: Spatial econometrics in real estate valuation

research_question: Does the staggered introduction of high-speed rail links between mid-sized cities and major metropolitan employment centers produce heterogeneous effects on housing prices along the gradient of commute time reduction, and does the equilibrium outcome reflect a Rosen-Roback spatial resorting of workers by skill that changes neighborhood income composition?
method: Difference-in-differences with continuous treatment intensity (commute time reduction in minutes) exploiting staggered HSR station openings across European countries (France, Germany, Spain) using a border-discontinuity design that compares outcomes in served versus unserved municipalities equidistant from existing rail lines, with demographic decomposition using administrative tax records to identify skill sorting.
data: UIC Railway Statistics and national rail authority timetables for station openings and travel times (1995–2022), national cadastral and notarial transaction registries for France (DVF), Germany (Gutachterausschuss), and Spain (Catastro), and Eurostat NUTS-3 labor market data with educational attainment breakdowns.
key_finding: A 30-minute commute reduction to a major employment hub is expected to raise housing prices 8–15% in previously isolated mid-sized cities, but the skill sorting mechanism implies income heterogeneity grows within treated municipalities as high-skilled in-migrants outbid existing residents in central neighborhoods.
novelty_rationale: European HSR effects have been studied at the aggregate level but no paper jointly identifies the commute-gradient mechanism with administrative skill-sorting evidence across multiple national systems using a border-discontinuity design that addresses the placement endogeneity specific to infrastructure research.
nn_title: The Value of Access to Rail Transit in a Congested City: Evidence from Housing P
assigned_label: Transportation Infrastructure Impact on Property Values

research_question: Do the governance structures of sovereign wealth funds—specifically whether they operate under parliamentary oversight, independent investment mandates, or direct ministerial control—systematically affect the prices they pay for global commercial real estate and the subsequent capital appreciation of those assets?
method: Cross-sectional panel regression of transaction-level commercial real estate acquisition prices on SWF governance quality scores constructed from the Linaburg-Maduell Transparency Index and IWG Santiago Principles compliance ratings, using a within-seller instrument based on seller's simultaneous sales to SWF and non-SWF buyers to control for asset quality, with long-run performance tracked through subsequent appraisal and transaction data.
data: Global SWF database of direct real estate acquisitions (Preqin and SWF Institute, 2000–2023), CoStar and RCA global transaction database for matched comparable sales, Linaburg-Maduell Transparency Index annual scores, and MSCI/IPD appraisal data for UK and Australian holdings where available.
key_finding: SWFs with low transparency scores are expected to pay 5–15% premiums over comparable non-SWF buyers in the same quarter, consistent with political mandate distortion, while high-transparency SWFs show no significant premium and earn superior risk-adjusted returns over 5-year holding periods.
novelty_rationale: Existing work on SWF real estate investment describes portfolio characteristics without connecting governance structure to pricing discipline and performance outcomes; this paper introduces a governance-to-pricing mechanism grounded in public finance theory that has no analog in the current corpus.
nn_title: Transparency, Integration, and the Cost of International Real Estate Investments
assigned_label: International real estate equity risk and returns

research_question: Does the introduction of biometric-based identity verification requirements for real estate transactions—mandated in several jurisdictions to combat money laundering—reduce foreign buyer activity and transaction prices in affected high-value market segments, providing causal evidence on the role of anonymity in luxury real estate pricing?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting FinCEN Geographic Targeting Order expansions across U.S. metropolitan areas (2016–2023) as a quasi-experiment requiring beneficial ownership disclosure for all-cash luxury transactions above jurisdictional thresholds, comparing price and volume dynamics above and below the threshold using a regression discontinuity in transaction value design nested within the DD framework.
data: FinCEN GTО coverage maps and effective dates, CoreLogic and DataQuick transaction records with deed-type and financing information, IRS FBAR foreign financial account reports at the MSA level (aggregated), and Treasury OFAC sanctions lists for cross-validation of buyer identity patterns.
key_finding: GTО expansion is expected to reduce all-cash transaction volume in affected luxury segments by 15–25% and prices by 3–8% in the immediate post-announcement period, with larger effects in metropolitan areas previously identified as money laundering hotspots (Miami, Manhattan, Los Angeles, San Francisco).
novelty_rationale: The foreign buyer tax literature focuses on transaction taxes and does not isolate the anonymity channel; using FinCEN GTО expansions to separate opacity-driven demand from fundamentals-driven foreign investment is methodologically novel and directly addresses the money laundering externality absent from the existing corpus.
nn_title: Gun‐ownership disclosure and localized home prices
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does exposure to wildfire smoke pollution—distinct from direct property destruction—generate persistent negative capitalization effects in residential real estate markets through chronic health risk perception, and how do these effects interact with flood risk disclosure to produce compounding climate risk discounts?
method: Hedonic panel regression using zip-code-daily air quality index readings from EPA monitoring stations to construct cumulative smoke-day exposure measures for each property over its inter-sale period, exploiting plausibly exogenous variation in smoke transport from fire locations determined by wind patterns using a rotational wind instrument, with interaction terms between smoke exposure and FEMA flood zone membership to test risk compounding.
data: EPA AirNow daily PM2.5 and AQI data at monitoring station level (2010–2023), NOAA HYSPLIT atmospheric transport model outputs for smoke source attribution, CoreLogic parcel-level repeat sales transaction data for Western and Southeastern U.S., and FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer for flood zone membership.
key_finding: Properties accumulating more than 30 smoke-days per inter-sale period are expected to sell at 2–6% discounts after controlling for fire damage risk, with the discount doubling for properties simultaneously located in FEMA Special Flood Hazard Areas, consistent with compounding climate risk perception that exceeds actuarially fair pricing.
novelty_rationale: The wildfire literature focuses on proximity to fire perimeters and direct destruction; no study isolates chronic smoke pollution as a separate housing price mechanism using atmospheric transport instruments, nor tests whether overlapping climate hazards produce compounding discounts beyond additive effects.
nn_title: Do Repeated Wildfires Change Homebuyers’ Demand for Homes in High-Risk Areas? A
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: Do housing markets exhibit heterogeneous price dynamics across the income distribution of buyers—specifically, does the return predictability documented for aggregate indexes arise almost entirely from the low-price-tier segment where search frictions and liquidity constraints are binding, while high-price-tier transactions follow near-random-walk dynamics consistent with more informationally efficient submarkets?
method: Quantile repeat-sales regression estimated separately across price quintiles using a panel of U.S. metropolitan areas, with predictability measured by the Sharpe ratio of a trading strategy based on lagged returns within each quintile, and market tightness instrumented by local employment shocks identified from Bartik shift-share at the MSA level to establish causal direction.
data: Zillow Research repeat-sales transaction microdata covering all U.S. zip codes (1996–2023) with transaction price deciles, BLS Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages for Bartik instrument construction, and Census Bureau American Community Survey housing value distributions for quintile cutoff construction.
key_finding: Low-price-tier housing is expected to exhibit serial correlation in returns three to five times larger than high-tier housing, with the entire predictable component concentrated below the 40th price percentile, implying that aggregate return predictability statistics substantially overstate opportunities for informed investors operating at market-clearing prices.
novelty_rationale: While return predictability and price tier dynamics have been studied separately, no paper tests the theoretical prediction that market efficiency varies monotonically across the price distribution using a causal identification strategy, nor links this to the search-and-matching literature on liquidity constraints as the mechanism.
nn_title: The Predictability of House Prices
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics, bubbles, and cycles

research_question: Does the staggered adoption of state-level right-to-repair legislation—which mandates that landlords allow tenants to make minor repairs and deduct costs from rent—causally affect rental housing maintenance investment, vacancy rates, and property values?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences with heterogeneity-robust estimators (Callaway-Sant'Anna), exploiting variation in adoption timing across states and comparing properties near state borders with divergent legal regimes, supplemented by a landlord cost-of-service model to separate mechanical from behavioral responses.
data: CoStar commercial lease and property data, American Housing Survey longitudinal files, Zillow rental listing data, state legislative records from the National Conference of State Legislatures, and county-level building permit data from the Census Bureau, covering 2000–2023.
key_finding: Right-to-repair laws plausibly reduce landlord deferred-maintenance problems in high-vacancy markets but may cause landlords to exit the rental supply in tight markets, producing heterogeneous effects ranging from rent reductions of 2–5% to vacancy increases of 1–3 percentage points depending on local supply elasticity.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus extensively studies rent control and tenant protection laws, no existing paper examines right-to-repair statutes as a distinct instrument that simultaneously affects maintenance investment, property values, and housing supply through a landlord moral hazard channel.
nn_title: What do economists know about the effect of rent control on housing maintenance?
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Do satellite-measured nighttime light intensity changes around newly constructed logistics hubs causally predict commercial real estate cap rate compression in surrounding industrial submarkets, and can this remote-sensing signal be operationalized as a real-time leading indicator for private-market appraisals?
method: Panel instrumental variables regression using exogenous variation in highway interchange construction approvals as an instrument for logistics hub location, combined with a forecasting horse race comparing nighttime light growth rates against traditional fundamentals (employment, vacancy) in predicting subsequent NPI industrial returns.
data: NOAA VIIRS nighttime light imagery (2012–2023 monthly), NCREIF Property Index industrial segment quarterly returns, CoStar industrial lease and cap rate data, Federal Highway Administration interchange construction records, and LEHD employment data.
key_finding: A one-standard-deviation increase in nighttime light growth within 5 km of a logistics hub predicts cap rate compression of 30–60 basis points over the following four quarters, with predictive accuracy superior to vacancy-based fundamentals and commensurate with private-market appraisal lags.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains no paper linking satellite remote-sensing data to private commercial real estate valuation or proposing nighttime luminosity as a real-time appraisal signal, representing an import of geospatial earth-observation methods entirely absent from the existing literature.
nn_title: Benchmarking Local Commercial Real Estate Returns: Statistics Meets Economics
assigned_label: Spatial econometrics in real estate valuation

research_question: Does the staggered implementation of municipal accessory dwelling unit (ADU) legalization ordinances causally increase total housing supply and reduce rent growth, and do spillover effects differ between owner-occupied and investor-owned parcels?
method: Difference-in-differences with continuous treatment intensity (share of parcels newly eligible under reformed zoning) using the universe of California municipal ADU ordinance adoption dates as the quasi-experiment, with heterogeneity analysis by parcel ownership type identified through deed records.
data: UC Berkeley Terner Center ADU permit database, California assessor parcel files linked to deed records (ownership type), Zillow observed rent index at the ZIP code level, Census Bureau building permit surveys, and municipal zoning ordinance adoption dates compiled from California's Housing Element filings, 2010–2023.
key_finding: ADU legalization increases permitted housing units by 8–15% on treated parcels, with rent reductions concentrated within half a mile of high-legalization zones and spillover supply effects roughly twice as large on investor-owned relative to owner-occupied parcels, suggesting investor responsiveness moderates aggregate supply elasticity.
novelty_rationale: Although the corpus includes one descriptive ADU paper, no study uses the staggered California ordinance rollout as a quasi-experiment to estimate causal supply elasticity and ownership-type heterogeneity, nor connects ADU policy to the broader housing supply elasticity literature reviewed in the corpus.
nn_title: The Effects of Rent Control Expansion on Tenants, Landlords, and Inequality: Evi
assigned_label: Rent Control Policy Effects and Outcomes

research_question: Do REIT managers with higher measured cognitive reflection ability—proxied by the gender and birth-year cohort composition of their graduate school graduating class, which predicts CRT performance in validated surveys—make systematically better property acquisition decisions as measured by subsequent asset-level NOI growth?
method: Two-stage instrumental variables strategy where a REIT manager's cohort-level cognitive reflection proxy (constructed from matched alumni databases and published CRT cohort norms) instruments for deal-level acquisition price premiums, with asset-level NOI outcomes from NCREIF as the dependent variable.
data: SNL Financial / S&P Global REIT executive biographical data linked to university alumni records, published cognitive reflection test norms by graduate school cohort from academic psychology studies, NCREIF property-level NOI and appraisal data, and REIT property transaction databases, 1995–2020.
key_finding: Managers from cohorts with higher predicted cognitive reflection scores pay acquisition premiums 4–8% lower on comparable assets and achieve NOI growth 1.5–2 percentage points higher over three years, consistent with superior ability to resist winner's curse dynamics in competitive bidding.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains papers on CEO overconfidence and optimism in REITs but none that import validated cognitive psychology constructs—specifically cognitive reflection—as executive ability proxies using a cohort-based instrumental variables design.
nn_title: CEO's age and acquisition behaviors of REITs
assigned_label: Corporate Governance and REIT Firm Value

research_question: Does the physical colocation of financial technology headquarters within a metropolitan area accelerate the diffusion of algorithmic mortgage pricing, compressing racial mortgage rate disparities faster in high-FinTech-density counties than in comparable low-density counties?
method: Shift-share instrumental variable exploiting the pre-existing geographic distribution of computer science graduates as a plausibly exogenous predictor of FinTech firm entry, applied to a county-year panel of HMDA mortgage originations decomposed by racial group and loan product type.
data: Home Mortgage Disclosure Act (HMDA) loan-level data 2007–2022, Crunchbase FinTech company founding and headquarters location data, IPEDS graduate-level computer science enrollment by institution and county, and the National Center for Education Statistics for historical CS graduation rates.
key_finding: A doubling of FinTech headquarters density reduces the Black–white mortgage rate spread by 8–15 basis points over five years in treated counties, with larger compression for online-originated loans and no detectable effect on loan approval disparities, suggesting algorithmic pricing narrows rate gaps without resolving access gaps.
novelty_rationale: The corpus includes extensive work on racial mortgage disparities and separately discusses screening and information technology, but no paper identifies FinTech geographic agglomeration as a causal mechanism for closing racial rate spreads using a shift-share design.
nn_title: Information Externalities, Neighborhood Characteristics and Home Mortgage Pricin
assigned_label: Mortgage lending: discrimination, pricing, and risk

research_question: Do households living in jurisdictions that adopted participatory budgeting programs—which allow citizens to vote on capital spending priorities—experience systematically higher residential property value appreciation attributable to improved neighborhood public goods alignment?
method: Fuzzy regression discontinuity design exploiting close mayoral election outcomes that determined participatory budgeting adoption in U.S. cities, instrumenting for program adoption with the electoral margin and comparing repeat-sales house price appreciation within half a mile of participatory budgeting projects versus comparable city-funded projects.
data: Participatory Budgeting Project database of U.S. municipal program adoptions and funded project locations, CoreLogic repeat-sales transaction records, city capital budget expenditure data from the Census of Governments, and election margin data from the MIT Election Data and Science Lab, 2000–2022.
key_finding: Participatory budgeting adoption increases annual house price appreciation by 0.5–1.2 percentage points within 500 meters of funded projects relative to identical municipal investments selected by elected officials, consistent with revealed-preference alignment between neighborhood spending and resident demand.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive work on the capitalization of public goods and school quality but no paper examines participatory budgeting—a governance innovation that endogenizes local public good selection—as a mechanism affecting housing prices, connecting political science and urban economics in a novel design.
nn_title: Heterogeneity in property tax capitalization: Evidence from municipalities in Wi
assigned_label: Property taxation effects on housing markets

research_question: Does the staggered adoption of electronic construction permitting systems across U.S. counties—which dramatically reduce permit processing times—causally increase new residential housing supply responsiveness to demand shocks, and does this effect interact with underlying land use regulation stringency?
method: Triple-difference estimator combining county-level electronic permitting adoption dates, Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index scores, and Saiz housing supply elasticity quartiles, with quarterly Census building permit approvals and housing starts as outcomes, controlling for county fixed effects and regional-by-quarter trends.
data: International City/County Management Association e-government survey identifying electronic permitting adoption years (available 2000–2020), Census Bureau Building Permits Survey monthly county-level data, Wharton Residential Land Use Regulatory Index, Saiz (2010) supply elasticity measures, and BLS local employment data.
key_finding: Electronic permitting adoption reduces average permit processing time by 20–35% and increases the supply elasticity of building permits with respect to house price growth by 0.15–0.30 in low-regulation counties, with near-zero effects in highly regulated jurisdictions where administrative bottlenecks are not the binding constraint.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus extensively debates housing supply elasticity and zoning, no paper exploits the administrative innovation of digital permitting as a source of exogenous variation in processing friction, and none documents its heterogeneous interaction with existing regulatory stringency.
nn_title: The local residential land use regulatory environment across U.S. housing market
assigned_label: Housing market dynamics, bubbles, and cycles

research_question: Does text-derived sentiment from commercial tenant earnings call transcripts—specifically language about remote-work policy, space utilization, and lease renewal intentions—predict subsequent office market rent changes and vacancy rates at the submarket level before these signals appear in broker survey data?
method: Panel predictive regression of quarterly CoStar office rent growth and vacancy changes on lagged sentence-level sentiment scores from S&P Global Transcripts using a fine-tuned BERT model trained on real estate language, with Diebold-Mariano out-of-sample forecast comparisons against CBRE/JLL broker survey forecasts.
data: S&P Global Market Intelligence earnings call transcripts for all U.S.-listed firms with identified office tenancy (2010–2023), CoStar submarket-level quarterly rent and vacancy data, CBRE and JLL published quarterly office market survey reports, and Trepp CMBS loan-level data for underwriting validation.
key_finding: Tenant earnings call sentiment predicts subsequent-quarter office submarket rent growth with out-of-sample R² of 0.12–0.18, leading broker survey signals by one to two quarters and providing significant incremental forecasting value in coastal gateway markets most affected by remote-work adoption post-2020.
novelty_rationale: The corpus includes papers on media sentiment and REIT conference call vocal cues but no paper harvests the demand side—tenant earnings calls—as a leading indicator for private commercial real estate market outcomes, representing a novel application of NLP to the office market forecasting problem.
nn_title: On the Relationship between Market Sentiment and Commercial Real Estate Performa
assigned_label: Real Estate Market Liquidity and Efficiency

research_question: Does the staggered introduction of automated fare collection systems on urban bus rapid transit lines—which enable continuous ridership data collection—causally increase the residential property value premium associated with BRT proximity by resolving information asymmetries about actual service quality?
method: Spatial difference-in-differences using BRT route openings interacted with automated fare collection system introduction dates as a two-dimensional treatment, with hedonic repeat-sales price regressions on properties within 800 meters of BRT stations relative to 800–2,000 meter control rings, using event-time specifications.
data: American Public Transportation Association transit agency census with technology adoption dates, Zillow and CoreLogic geocoded residential transaction records, GTFS transit schedule data for BRT routes, Census Bureau American Community Survey commuting data, and National Transit Database ridership statistics, 2005–2022.
key_finding: BRT proximity generates a baseline price premium of 3–6%, but this premium increases by an additional 1.5–3 percentage points following automated fare collection adoption, consistent with information-based capitalization of revealed ridership quality signals rather than pure infrastructure proximity effects.
novelty_rationale: The corpus contains extensive work on transit capitalization in house prices but no paper identifies information revelation about transit quality—through ridership data technology—as a distinct mechanism producing time-varying capitalization, separating information from pure infrastructure effects.
nn_title: The capitalization of metro rail access in urban housing markets
assigned_label: Transportation Infrastructure Impact on Property Values

research_question: Do institutional investors in the single-family rental market impose differential rent burdens on minority households relative to observationally identical small landlords, and does this gap widen during local economic contractions through asymmetric lease renewal pricing power?
method: Matched differences-in-differences using CoreLogic deed records to identify institutional vs. small-landlord ownership of otherwise identical single-family properties within the same Census tract, with Zillow observed rent data and American Community Survey tenant demographic estimates, exploiting variation in local institutional market share as a continuous treatment.
data: CoreLogic property ownership and transaction records (2012–2023) identifying investor type by deed grantor characteristics and portfolio size, Zillow Observed Rent Index at ZIP code level, American Community Survey 5-year estimates of tract-level racial composition and renter shares, and Local Area Unemployment Statistics for business cycle interactions.
key_finding: Institutional landlords charge rents 4–8% higher than matched small landlords for comparable properties, with a racial gap of 2–4 additional percentage points in majority-minority tracts, and this differential expands by 1.5–2.5 percentage points during local unemployment shocks consistent with institutional investors exploiting reduced mobility options.
novelty_rationale: While the corpus examines investor effects on house prices and contains one paper on corporate landlord behavior in NYC multifamily, no study uses matched deed-record methods to identify racial rent differentials attributable to institutional single-family landlords or documents their countercyclical price discrimination behavior.
nn_title: Do Wall Street Landlords Undermine Renters’ Welfare?
assigned_label: Racial disparities in homeownership and housing outcomes

research_question: Does the staggered legalization of sports betting across U.S. states causally affect commercial real estate values and vacancy rates in entertainment districts, stadium-adjacent zones, and casino markets?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting the state-by-state rollout of legal sports betting following the 2018 PASPA repeal, using Census tract-level commercial property transaction data and CoStar vacancy records to estimate treatment effects on retail and hospitality real estate within defined radii of licensed sportsbook venues.
data: CoStar commercial transaction and vacancy data (2015–2024), MSCI/RCA deal-level records, American Gaming Association state-by-state legalization dates, and Census LODES employment data for entertainment-adjacent CBSAs.
key_finding: Legal sports betting legalization could generate positive externalities for nearby hospitality and bar-restaurant real estate (5–15% value uplift) while producing ambiguous effects for casino-adjacent properties facing substitution, with effects concentrated in dense urban entertainment districts.
novelty_rationale: No published study has linked the plausibly exogenous geographic rollout of legal sports betting to commercial real estate market outcomes, despite the policy's scale and the active debate over entertainment district revitalization.
nn_title: Strip Clubs, “Secondary Effects” and Residential Property Prices
assigned_label: Real Estate Finance Research Frontiers

research_question: Do mortgage servicers exhibit racial disparities in forbearance offer timing and modification terms during aggregate income shocks, and does servicer business model heterogeneity—bank versus nonbank—moderate these disparities?
method: Hazard model analysis of forbearance entry and exit timing exploiting the COVID-19 CARES Act as an exogenous shock, with loan-level Black Knight McDash data matched to servicer type (bank vs. nonbank) and borrower race imputed via BISG methodology, using servicer fixed effects to isolate within-servicer racial gaps.
data: Black Knight McDash loan-level performance data (2020–2023), HMDA origination files with borrower demographic information, CFPB nonbank servicer registry, and Urban Institute servicer classification data covering approximately 25 million active loans.
key_finding: Nonbank servicers may exhibit larger racial disparities in forbearance processing speed and modification offer rates than bank servicers, driven by call-center capacity constraints and weaker regulatory oversight, with minority borrowers facing 15–30 day longer resolution times.
novelty_rationale: Existing work on servicer discrimination focuses on modification outcomes rather than process timing, and no study has used the CARES Act forbearance episode to identify servicer-type-by-race interaction effects on resolution speed.
nn_title: What Drives Racial and Ethnic Differences in High-Cost Mortgages? The Role of Hi
assigned_label: Mortgage lending: discrimination, pricing, and risk

research_question: Does proximity to large urban vertical farms and controlled-environment agriculture facilities generate measurable price externalities on surrounding commercial and residential real estate, and do these effects differ by facility visibility and local food-access context?
method: Hedonic difference-in-differences using repeat-sales and cross-sectional residential and commercial property transactions within graduated rings around all identifiable urban vertical farm openings in the U.S. since 2010, exploiting the staggered facility opening dates as quasi-experimental variation.
data: CoreLogic residential transaction files, CoStar commercial transaction data, Vertical Farm Database (Cornell/USDA) providing facility addresses and opening dates, USDA Food Access Research Atlas for food desert classification, and Google Street View imagery for facility visibility scoring.
key_finding: Visible urban vertical farms likely generate modest positive amenity externalities (2–5%) on nearby residential properties in food-desert contexts while having negligible or mildly negative industrial spillover effects on adjacent commercial parcels due to odor and traffic concerns.
novelty_rationale: Urban vertical farming is a rapidly scaling land use whose real estate externalities have never been empirically studied, creating a genuine gap at the intersection of urban economics, food systems, and environmental amenity valuation.
nn_title: The Grass Isn’t Always Greener: How Crime Influences Property Values Near Urban
assigned_label: Environmental Contamination and Property Values

research_question: Do corporate lease renegotiations triggered by financial distress reveal systematic landlord bargaining power heterogeneity, and does this heterogeneity predict subsequent REIT property income volatility?
method: Event study and panel regression linking 8-K and 10-K REIT filings disclosing tenant lease modifications to subsequent net operating income volatility, using textual analysis of SEC filings to classify renegotiation outcomes, and exploiting plausibly exogenous tenant distress events (Chapter 11 filings) as instruments for renegotiation incidence.
data: SEC EDGAR full-text search of REIT 10-K and 8-K filings (2000–2023), UCLA LoPucki Bankruptcy Research Database for tenant Chapter 11 events, NCREIF property-level NOI data, and SNL Real Estate (now S&P Global) for REIT property-tenant concentration.
key_finding: REITs with higher tenant concentration facing distress-driven renegotiations likely experience 10–20% greater subsequent NOI volatility, with landlord bargaining power (proxied by submarket vacancy) moderating rent concession depth and predicting equity return variance.
novelty_rationale: While lease renegotiation and REIT income are studied separately, no paper combines textual extraction of lease modification terms with instrumental identification of distress-driven renegotiations to measure landlord bargaining power and its income consequences.
nn_title: Financial Flexibility and Manager–Shareholder Conflict: Evidence from REITs
assigned_label: REIT Capital Structure and Financing Decisions

research_question: Does the rollout of municipal street-level autonomous vehicle testing corridors generate capitalization effects on commercial real estate along designated routes through changes in pedestrian traffic, parking demand, and perceived accessibility?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered designation of AV testing corridors across U.S. cities since 2017 (Pittsburgh, Phoenix, San Francisco, Austin), comparing CoStar commercial lease rates and vacancy within 200 meters of designated corridors to matched control corridors using propensity-score balancing on pre-treatment pedestrian counts and land use.
data: CoStar commercial lease transaction data, city AV corridor designation ordinances and dates (publicly available municipal records), Replica/Streetlight pedestrian mobility data, OpenStreetMap land use classifications, and parking meter revenue data from municipal open data portals.
key_finding: AV corridor designation likely reduces parking-adjacent retail rents by 3–8% while increasing rents for transit-oriented food and service retail by 4–10%, reflecting a revaluation of accessibility that parallels transit-proximity capitalization patterns documented in the light rail literature.
novelty_rationale: No study has examined how autonomous vehicle infrastructure designations—distinct from ride-hailing or transit—affect commercial real estate values, despite the policy relevance as cities plan AV deployment at scale.
nn_title: The Impact of Transit Corridors on Residential Property Values
assigned_label: Transportation Infrastructure Impact on Property Values

research_question: Do residential mortgage lenders adjust loan pricing and origination volumes in response to municipal-level heat ordinance adoption—specifically extreme heat work restrictions and cooling center mandates—as signals of future climate-related property risk?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences using the adoption dates of municipal heat ordinances in Sun Belt metros matched to HMDA loan-level origination data, estimating effects on denial rates, loan-to-value ratios, interest rate spreads, and origination volumes for properties in high heat-exposure census tracts versus comparable low-exposure tracts in the same MSA.
data: HMDA loan-level origination data (2010–2023), municipal heat ordinance adoption dates compiled from National League of Cities and Westlaw legal database, EPA EnviroAtlas heat island intensity rasters at 30-meter resolution, and FEMA National Risk Index heat scores at census tract level.
key_finding: Municipal heat ordinance adoption signals localized physical climate risk to lenders, potentially reducing origination volumes by 5–12% and increasing spreads by 15–40 basis points in the highest heat-exposure quartile tracts, consistent with climate risk repricing but inconsistent with full information efficiency.
novelty_rationale: Existing climate-mortgage research focuses on flood and hurricane risk; this is the first design exploiting heat ordinances as plausibly exogenous policy signals to identify lender behavioral responses to a distinct and rapidly growing physical climate hazard.
nn_title: Climate risk in mortgage markets: Evidence from Hurricanes Harvey and Irma
assigned_label: Natural Disasters, Climate Risk, and Real Estate Pricing

research_question: Does the geographic footprint of same-day delivery fulfillment networks by grocery e-commerce platforms—specifically dark store locations—generate negative externalities on traditional grocery-anchored retail center values and positive externalities on adjacent industrial real estate?
method: Hedonic difference-in-differences using the staggered entry of dark store openings (Instacart, DoorDash, Gopuff, Amazon Fresh) across U.S. metros to estimate effects on capitalization rates and rental rates of grocery-anchored strip centers within competing trade areas and industrial properties within two miles, identified from CoStar data with facility-type flags.
data: CoStar commercial transaction and lease data with property type classifications, dark store opening dates and addresses compiled from local business permit records and industry databases (Edge by Ascential, Placer.ai), and foot traffic data from SafeGraph (now Spectus) for retail center visits.
key_finding: Dark store openings within a 1.5-mile trade area likely reduce grocery-anchored retail center cap rates by 20–50 basis points (indicating higher risk pricing) while increasing light industrial rents by 4–8%, with effects concentrated in dense urban markets where delivery demand is highest.
novelty_rationale: While e-commerce logistics effects on industrial real estate are studied broadly, the specific competitive externality of dark grocery stores on adjacent retail formats and the simultaneous industrial uplift has not been empirically identified using staggered entry as quasi-experimental variation.
nn_title: Retail Concentration and Shopping Center Rents—A Comparison of Two Cities
assigned_label: Shopping center economics and retail performance

research_question: Does the staggered adoption of mandatory building electrification ordinances in U.S. cities causally affect the relative pricing of gas-heated versus all-electric residential properties, and does this effect vary by housing vintage and neighborhood income?
method: Repeat-sales difference-in-differences exploiting the sequential adoption of building electrification mandates across cities (Berkeley 2019, San Jose 2020, New York City 2021 Local Law 154, etc.), comparing price trajectories of all-electric versus gas-heated properties using permit records linked to CoreLogic transaction files, with city-by-quarter fixed effects and heating fuel type identified from building permit and assessor data.
data: CoreLogic property transaction files with assessor characteristics including heating fuel type (2015–2024), municipal electrification ordinance adoption dates from Rocky Mountain Institute's Building Electrification Initiative tracker, residential building permit records from Census Bureau Building Permits Survey, and RECS energy characteristic data for validation.
key_finding: Electrification mandate adoption likely generates a 3–7% appreciation premium for already-compliant all-electric homes in treated cities relative to synthetic controls, with larger effects in higher-income neighborhoods where compliance costs are relatively lower and green preferences stronger.
novelty_rationale: Building electrification mandates represent a novel and rapidly spreading climate policy with direct implications for property value heterogeneity, yet no published study has used the staggered rollout to identify the capitalization effect of heating fuel compliance status.
nn_title: Clean Electricity, Dirty Electricity: The Effect on Local House Prices
assigned_label: Green Building Features and Property Valuation

research_question: Does the spatial concentration of remote work among high-income knowledge workers—measured by mobile device dwelling patterns—predict within-city residential price gradient flattening, and has this relationship changed the classical rent-distance relationship in monocentric cities?
