method: Panel regression of ZIP-code-level house price gradients (distance-to-CBD slopes estimated quarterly from CoreLogic transaction data) on within-ZIP remote work intensity measured by device dwelling time from Spectus/SafeGraph workplace mobility data, using the COVID-19 pandemic shock as an instrumental variable for remote work adoption and applying a spatial panel model with MSA and quarter fixed effects.
data: CoreLogic residential transaction files (2018–2024) for price gradient estimation, Spectus (formerly SafeGraph) device-level dwelling patterns aggregated to ZIP code for remote work intensity, American Community Survey occupation-by-ZIP remote-compatible employment shares for instrument construction, and Census LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics.
key_finding: A 10-percentage-point increase in remote work intensity likely flattens the within-MSA price-distance gradient by 8–15%, with monocentric cities exhibiting larger gradient compression than polycentric metros, consistent with a structural shift in household location demand that partially persists post-pandemic.
novelty_rationale: While remote work and urban prices are studied separately, directly linking high-frequency device-based remote work intensity to the empirical estimation of Alonso-Muth-Mills rent-distance gradients across a large panel of cities and testing gradient flattening formally has not been done at this granularity.
nn_title: Housing Price Gradients in a Region with One Dominating Center
assigned_label: Spatial econometrics in real estate valuation

research_question: Does the appointment of economists with real estate expertise to Federal Reserve regional bank boards—as distinguished from financial sector or nonfinancial business directors—systematically affect local mortgage credit supply and housing price dynamics through bank supervisory channels?
method: Difference-in-differences exploiting the staggered appointment of academic real estate economists versus other director types to Federal Reserve Class B and C board positions (publicly disclosed), linking these appointments to HMDA county-level mortgage origination volumes and FHFA house price index changes in the Fed district footprints, using a director appointment event study and panel regression with district and year fixed effects.
data: Federal Reserve Board public director appointment records (all 12 Federal Reserve Banks, 1994–2023) from annual reports and Federal Register filings, HMDA loan origination data aggregated to county-year, FHFA All-Transactions House Price Index at MSA level, and Call Report data for member bank lending behavior within districts.
key_finding: Districts gaining real estate expert directors likely experience 4–9% higher mortgage origination growth in the subsequent two years, consistent with expertise-informed supervisory guidance reducing excessive credit tightening, with effects concentrated in periods of macroprudential policy uncertainty.
novelty_rationale: No research has connected Federal Reserve board director expertise composition to local mortgage and housing market outcomes, opening a novel intersection of central bank governance, supervisory economics, and real estate finance with genuine implications for macroprudential policy design.
nn_title: REAL ESTATE CREDIT CONTROLS AS A SELECTIVE INSTRUMENT OF FEDERAL RESERVE POLICY*
assigned_label: Interest rates, credit, and housing cycles

research_question: Do real estate auction participants exhibit systematic anchoring to the first bid in sequential sealed-bid land auctions, and does this anchor persist across repeated auctions of homogeneous parcels in the same market?
method: Regression discontinuity and time-series analysis of sequential auction outcomes, exploiting variation in opening bid values across otherwise comparable land parcels to identify causal anchoring effects on final transaction prices, directly paralleling the Macao auction anchoring design applied to government land tender data.
data: Government land auction records from Hong Kong, Singapore, or mainland Chinese municipal land bureaus — all of which publish parcel-level tender outcomes including opening bids, final prices, bidder counts, parcel characteristics, and auction sequence, and have been used extensively in published real estate research.
key_finding: Final transaction prices are plausibly 3–12% higher when opening bids are set at the upper range of the announced reserve band, with the anchoring effect attenuating but not disappearing across repeated auctions of similar parcels in the same quarter, suggesting experienced developers remain susceptible to numerical anchors even in high-stakes, repeated market settings.
novelty_rationale: While anchoring in real estate is discussed theoretically, no study has applied the repeated-homogeneous-object auction design from experimental psychology to identify whether anchoring persists across sequential professional land tenders, a setting where learning and high stakes should theoretically eliminate the bias.
nn_title: Strategic default, workout, and commercial mortgage valuation
assigned_label: Real estate agents, pricing, and market behavior

research_question: Does the staggered introduction of state-level residential eviction expungement laws—which seal or delete eviction court records from tenant screening databases—causally improve housing access and stability for previously evicted renters?
method: Staggered difference-in-differences design exploiting variation in timing of eviction expungement law adoption across states, using a callaway-Sant'Anna heterogeneity-robust estimator to identify effects on renter mobility, subsequent eviction rates, rent burdens, and neighborhood quality of placement.
data: Princeton Eviction Lab state-level eviction filing and judgment records (2000–2023) merged with HUD Picture of Subsidized Households, American Community Survey public-use microdata, and state legislative adoption dates compiled from Westlaw and NCSL databases.
key_finding: Expungement laws are plausibly expected to reduce subsequent eviction rates by 10–20% and enable access to higher-quality neighborhoods, but may also raise landlord screening costs leading to tighter rental markets in low-income segments.
novelty_rationale: While eviction research is growing rapidly, no study has used a quasi-experimental design to estimate the causal downstream housing market consequences of expungement laws specifically, which represent a novel and rapidly diffusing policy lever distinct from eviction moratoria or right-to-counsel laws.
nn_title: Interest Rate Pass-Through: Mortgage Rates, Household Consumption, and Voluntary
assigned_label: Residential-employment location choice interactions
